1	"""End-to-end rate-limit tests over the wired app (#725, ADR 0035).
2	
3	TestClient over `backend.app.main.app` — no DB fixtures needed: the limiter
4	counts *before* routing, so 404/401/whatever from the inner route suffices (we
5	only assert the 429 boundary, never the inner status). The `limiter` fixture
6	enables limiting with low limits + an injected in-memory store and freezes time
7	so a window boundary can't flake the counts.
8	"""
9	
10	from __future__ import annotations
11	
12	import logging
13	from collections.abc import Iterator, Sequence
14	
15	import pytest
16	from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
17	
18	from backend.app.core import rate_limit
19	from backend.app.core.config import get_settings
20	from backend.app.core.rate_limit import InMemoryStore, set_store_for_testing
21	from backend.app.main import app
22	
23	# A path with no route: the limiter runs pre-routing, so the inner 404 is
24	# irrelevant — we assert only whether the request was throttled.
25	PROBE = "/api/v1/__rl_probe__"
26	_FROZEN = 1_000_000.0
27	
28	
29	@pytest.fixture
30	def client() -> Iterator[TestClient]:
31	    """Plain client — rate limiting OFF (suite default RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED=false)."""
32	    with TestClient(app) as c:
33	        yield c
34	
35	
36	@pytest.fixture
37	def limiter(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> Iterator[TestClient]:
38	    monkeypatch.setenv("RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED", "true")
39	    monkeypatch.setenv("RATE_LIMIT_UNAUTHENTICATED_PER_MINUTE", "3")
40	    monkeypatch.setenv("RATE_LIMIT_AUTHENTICATED_PER_MINUTE", "5")
41	    monkeypatch.setenv("RATE_LIMIT_WEBHOOK_PER_MINUTE", "2")
42	    get_settings.cache_clear()
43	    # Freeze time so every request in a test lands in one fixed window.
44	    monkeypatch.setattr(rate_limit, "_now", lambda: _FROZEN)
45	    set_store_for_testing(InMemoryStore(clock=lambda: _FROZEN))
46	    with TestClient(app) as c:
47	        yield c
48	
49	
50	@pytest.fixture
51	def ip_ceiling_limiter(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> Iterator[TestClient]:
52	    """Low per-IP bearer ceiling, high token cap — exercises the rotated-token
53	    backstop (`rate_limit_ip_per_minute`) without the per-token bucket firing."""
54	    monkeypatch.setenv("RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED", "true")
55	    monkeypatch.setenv("RATE_LIMIT_AUTHENTICATED_PER_MINUTE", "100")  # token cap out of the way
56	    monkeypatch.setenv("RATE_LIMIT_IP_PER_MINUTE", "4")  # per-IP ceiling across all tokens
57	    monkeypatch.setenv("RATE_LIMIT_WEBHOOK_PER_MINUTE", "50")
58	    get_settings.cache_clear()
59	    monkeypatch.setattr(rate_limit, "_now", lambda: _FROZEN)
60	    set_store_for_testing(InMemoryStore(clock=lambda: _FROZEN))
61	    with TestClient(app) as c:
62	        yield c
63	
64	
65	def test_rotating_bearer_hits_ip_ceiling(ip_ceiling_limiter: TestClient) -> None:
66	    # A fresh random bearer each request → a fresh tok bucket every time, so the
67	    # per-token cap never fires; the per-IP ipall ceiling (4) closes the bypass.
68	    for i in range(4):
69	        h = {"Authorization": f"Bearer rotate-{i}"}
70	        assert ip_ceiling_limiter.get(PROBE, headers=h).status_code != 429
71	    resp = ip_ceiling_limiter.get(PROBE, headers={"Authorization": "Bearer rotate-final"})
72	    assert resp.status_code == 429
73	    assert resp.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"] == "4"  # the IP ceiling, reported
74	
75	
76	def test_two_distinct_tokens_share_ip_ceiling(ip_ceiling_limiter: TestClient) -> None:
77	    ha = {"Authorization": "Bearer token-AAA"}
78	    hb = {"Authorization": "Bearer token-BBB"}
79	    assert ip_ceiling_limiter.get(PROBE, headers=ha).status_code != 429
80	    assert ip_ceiling_limiter.get(PROBE, headers=hb).status_code != 429
81	    assert ip_ceiling_limiter.get(PROBE, headers=ha).status_code != 429
82	    assert ip_ceiling_limiter.get(PROBE, headers=hb).status_code != 429  # 4 total, at ceiling
83	    # 5th bearer request from the same IP (either token) → ipall ceiling 429.
84	    assert ip_ceiling_limiter.get(PROBE, headers=ha).status_code == 429
85	
86	
87	def test_webhook_with_bearer_not_counted_against_ip_ceiling(ip_ceiling_limiter: TestClient) -> None:
88	    path = "/api/v1/orchestration/events/adf"
89	    h = {"Authorization": "Bearer token-AAA"}
90	    # 6 webhook posts > the ipall ceiling (4); they stay in the webhook class
91	    # (cap 50) and never touch ipall, so none are throttled.
92	    for _ in range(6):
93	        assert ip_ceiling_limiter.post(path, headers=h).status_code != 429
94	    # And a subsequent bearer request is ipall #1, not #7 → still allowed.
95	    assert ip_ceiling_limiter.get(PROBE, headers=h).status_code != 429
96	
97	
98	def test_single_token_still_capped_at_authenticated_limit(limiter: TestClient) -> None:
99	    # Default `limiter`: AUTH cap 5, IP ceiling default 1200 (out of the way).
100	    # The per-token bucket stays intact and independent of the higher ceiling.
101	    h = {"Authorization": "Bearer solo-token"}
102	    for _ in range(5):
103	        assert limiter.get(PROBE, headers=h).status_code != 429
104	    resp = limiter.get(PROBE, headers=h)
105	    assert resp.status_code == 429
106	    assert resp.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"] == "5"  # the token limit, not the ceiling
107	
108	
109	def _structlog_events(records: list[logging.LogRecord]) -> list[dict[str, object]]:
110	    out: list[dict[str, object]] = []
111	    for rec in records:
112	        evt = getattr(rec, "_record", None) or rec.__dict__.get("event_dict")
113	        if evt is None and isinstance(rec.msg, dict):
114	            evt = rec.msg
115	        if isinstance(evt, dict):
116	            out.append(evt)
117	    return out
118	
119	
120	# ───────────────────────── 1. full 429 contract ─────────────────────────
121	
122	
123	def test_unauth_429_at_limit_plus_one_full_contract(limiter: TestClient) -> None:
124	    for _ in range(3):  # UNAUTH limit = 3
125	        assert limiter.get(PROBE).status_code != 429
126	    resp = limiter.get(PROBE)
127	    assert resp.status_code == 429
128	    body = resp.json()
129	    assert body["error"]["code"] == "rate_limited"
130	    retry = body["error"]["detail"]["retry_after_seconds"]
131	    assert isinstance(retry, int)
132	    assert 1 <= retry <= 60
133	    assert resp.headers["Retry-After"] == str(retry)
134	    assert resp.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"] == "3"
135	    assert resp.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"] == "0"
136	    # 429 still exits through request_id_middleware (innermost-limiter design).
137	    assert resp.headers.get("X-Request-ID")
138	
139	
140	# ───────────────────────── 2. per-token separation ─────────────────────────
141	
142	
143	def test_per_token_buckets_are_independent(limiter: TestClient) -> None:
144	    ha = {"Authorization": "Bearer token-AAA"}
145	    for _ in range(5):  # AUTH limit = 5
146	        assert limiter.get(PROBE, headers=ha).status_code != 429
147	    assert limiter.get(PROBE, headers=ha).status_code == 429
148	
149	    # A different token is a fresh bucket.
150	    hb = {"Authorization": "Bearer token-BBB"}
151	    assert limiter.get(PROBE, headers=hb).status_code != 429
152	
153	    # And none of those bearer requests touched the unauth IP bucket.
154	    for _ in range(3):  # UNAUTH limit = 3, still full
155	        assert limiter.get(PROBE).status_code != 429
156	    assert limiter.get(PROBE).status_code == 429
157	
158	
159	# ───────────────────────── 3. webhook class ─────────────────────────
160	
161	
162	def test_webhook_class_is_tighter_and_ip_keyed_despite_bearer(limiter: TestClient) -> None:
163	    path = "/api/v1/orchestration/events/adf"
164	    headers = {"Authorization": "Bearer token-AAA"}  # bearer must NOT switch it off ip-keying
165	    assert limiter.post(path, headers=headers).status_code != 429
166	    assert limiter.post(path, headers=headers).status_code != 429
167	    assert limiter.post(path, headers=headers).status_code == 429  # WEBHOOK limit = 2
168	
169	
170	# ───────────────────────── 4/5. exemptions ─────────────────────────
171	
172	
173	def test_healthz_is_exempt(limiter: TestClient) -> None:
174	    for _ in range(20):
175	        assert limiter.get("/healthz").status_code == 200
176

1	from functools import lru_cache
2	from typing import Literal
3	
4	from pydantic import Field
5	from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict
6	
7	
8	class Settings(BaseSettings):
9	    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
10	        # App config lives in .env.app (host dev reads it directly; compose injects
11	        # it into the api/worker containers via env_file). The root .env is
12	        # compose/infra-only (POSTGRES_*, VITE_*) and is NOT read here. extra=forbid
13	        # catches typo'd/stale keys — the split keeps those infra keys from tripping
14	        # it. See #209.
15	        env_file=".env.app",
16	        env_file_encoding="utf-8",
17	        extra="forbid",
18	        case_sensitive=False,
19	    )
20	
21	    environment: Literal["dev", "staging", "prod"] = "dev"
22	    log_level: Literal["DEBUG", "INFO", "WARNING", "ERROR", "CRITICAL"] = "INFO"
23	
24	    # No DB credentials embedded in code. The real URL (with password) comes from
25	    # the environment: docker-compose and CI set DATABASE_URL; local host dev gets
26	    # it from .env (setup.sh bootstraps + exports it). This default is only a
27	    # credential-less placeholder for the no-env case.
28	    database_url: str = Field(default="postgresql+psycopg2://localhost:5432/dataq")
29	    redis_url: str = Field(default="redis://localhost:6379/0")
30	
31	    applicationinsights_connection_string: str | None = None
32	
33	    # Generic OTLP/HTTP exporter endpoint (#589) — the standard OTel contract
34	    # (OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT). When set, spans AND logs also export to this
35	    # OTLP consumer (Grafana/Tempo, Jaeger, Datadog, …) via the OTLP/HTTP exporter,
36	    # with base-endpoint semantics (`/v1/traces` + `/v1/logs` appended). The Azure
37	    # Monitor exporter (APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING) is just one backend
38	    # behind the same seam (ADR 0010); both may be set at once — that's the parity
39	    # check (same trace/log in App Insights AND a local collector). Neither set ⇒
40	    # telemetry is off. The standard sibling env vars (OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS,
41	    # _TIMEOUT, …) are read by the exporter itself.
42	    otel_exporter_otlp_endpoint: str | None = None
43	
44	    # OpenLineage emission (ADR 0034, #758) — dark by default. When
45	    # OPENLINEAGE_URL is set (and OPENLINEAGE_DISABLED is not truthy) the run
46	    # lifecycle emits START/COMPLETE/FAIL/ABORT RunEvents (with DQ facets) to that
47	    # HTTP receiver (Marquez, an OpenLineage collector, …). Typed here — not read
48	    # from raw os.environ — so a value in `.env.app` (which the process env never
49	    # sees) still activates emission. The library-owned advanced transports
50	    # (OPENLINEAGE__TRANSPORT__* / OPENLINEAGE_CONFIG) stay in raw env, read by the
51	    # client itself; those are the only OpenLineage vars NOT surfaced here.
52	    #   OPENLINEAGE_URL=http://marquez:5000
53	    openlineage_url: str | None = None
54	    openlineage_disabled: bool = False
55	
56	    # Lineage catalog pull (ADR 0034, #762) — dark by default. The `LineageProvider`
57	    # seam pulls a lineage graph from a governance catalog and caches it into
58	    # `lineage_edges` (source='marquez'). Unset `lineage_provider` → no pull (the beat
59	    # task no-ops). Only `marquez` is implemented; DataHub/OpenMetadata/Purview are
60	    # deferred behind the same seam. Typed here (not raw os.environ) so a `.env.app`
61	    # value activates it. `marquez_url` is the reference server's base URL.
62	    #   LINEAGE_PROVIDER=marquez
63	    #   MARQUEZ_URL=http://marquez:5000
64	    lineage_provider: str = ""
65	    marquez_url: str | None = None
66	
67	    sample_failures_retention_days: int = 30
68	
69	    # Stuck-run reaper (#309): a run committed `queued` (before `send_task`) — or
70	    # left `running` by a worker that died mid-execution — past this age is driven
71	    # to terminal `failed` by the beat janitor so it can't linger forever. Must
72	    # comfortably exceed the longest plausible suite run so a slow-but-alive run is
73	    # never reaped (a false reap self-corrects when the worker later commits its
74	    # real outcome, but would emit a spurious alert).
75	    stuck_run_threshold_minutes: int = 60
76	
77	    # Orphan-asset sweep (#770, ADR 0034 — "asset rows accrete; last_seen + a
78	    # sweep, not deletes, is the cleanup posture"). An asset whose `last_seen`
79	    # hasn't advanced in this many days AND that no suite/run/lineage_edge (and,
80	    # once #761 lands, incident) still references is deleted by the beat janitor.
81	    # Deliberately generous — must comfortably outlive the slowest suite schedule
82	    # and the lineage-refresh poll cadence, or a legitimately-live asset would be
83	    # swept and immediately re-created on the next refresh. <=0 disables the sweep.
84	    asset_orphan_retention_days: int = 30
85	
86	    azure_tenant_id: str | None = None
87	    azure_api_client_id: str | None = None
88	    azure_spa_client_id: str | None = None
89	    azure_api_scope: str = "user_impersonation"
90	
91	    # Allow guest (B2B / external) identities in the tenant to authenticate.
92	    # Default off (fastapi-azure-auth's own secure default): the token validator
93	    # rejects guests with 403 "Guest users not allowed". Enable for deployments
94	    # whose legitimate users sign in with a guest account (e.g. a personal
95	    # Microsoft account invited into the tenant). Still bounded by tenant
96	    # membership + the API scope; orthogonal to WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS.
97	    azure_allow_guest_users: bool = False
98

2	
3	Pure — no TestClient / DB. Policy resolution, client-IP extraction, the two
4	stores, and the warn-once fail-open stamp. The middleware wired into the app is
5	exercised end-to-end in `backend/tests/api/test_rate_limiting.py`.
6	"""

27	def _settings() -> Settings:

31	def _make_request(
32	    headers: dict[str, str] | None = None,
33	    client: tuple[str, int] | None = ("1.2.3.4", 5000),
34	) -> Request:
35	    raw = [(k.lower().encode(), v.encode()) for k, v in (headers or {}).items()]
36	    scope: dict[str, object] = {"type": "http", "headers": raw, "client": client}
37	    return Request(scope)
38

43	def test_webhook_prefix_wins_even_with_bearer() -> None:
44	    s = _settings()
45	    cls, limit, key = _resolve_policy("/api/v1/orchestration/events/adf", "sometoken", "9.9.9.9", s)
46	    assert cls == "webhook"
47	    assert limit == s.rate_limit_webhook_per_minute
48	    assert key == "ip:9.9.9.9"  # per-IP even though a bearer was present
49	
50	
51	def test_bearer_keys_by_sha256_prefix() -> None:
52	    s = _settings()
53	    cls, limit, key = _resolve_policy("/api/v1/suites", "sometoken", "9.9.9.9", s)
54	    assert cls == "default"
55	    assert limit == s.rate_limit_authenticated_per_minute
56	    assert key.startswith("tok:")
57	    digest = key.removeprefix("tok:")
58	    assert len(digest) == 32
59	    assert all(c in "0123456789abcdef" for c in digest)
60	    assert digest == hashlib.sha256(b"sometoken").hexdigest()[:32]
61	
62	
63	def test_raw_token_never_in_key() -> None:
64	    _, _, key = _resolve_policy("/api/v1/suites", "super-secret-token", "9.9.9.9", _settings())
65	    assert "super-secret-token" not in key
66	
67	
68	def test_no_bearer_keys_by_ip() -> None:
69	    s = _settings()
70	    cls, limit, key = _resolve_policy("/api/v1/suites", None, "9.9.9.9", s)
71	    assert cls == "unauth"
72	    assert limit == s.rate_limit_unauthenticated_per_minute
73	    assert key == "ip:9.9.9.9"

82	    assert _client_ip(req, 1) == "9.9.9.9"

# Formats the profiler can actually parse. NOT redundant with
# flatfile.format_from_path (which only recognises path extensions): this also
# validates the caller's `explicit` file_format override (an arbitrary string),
# and is deliberately a *subset* of recognised formats — a format can be
# recognised by path yet unsupported here, which should still 422 (#147).
_SUPPORTED_FORMATS = {"csv", "parquet"}
# Flat-file profiling reads at most this many rows — stats are over the sample.
_SAMPLE_ROWS = 100_000

# A plain SQL identifier: letter/underscore start, then letters/digits/_/$ (the
# Snowflake unquoted-identifier set). Anything else (spaces, quotes, dots, etc.)
# is refused — it can't be made injection-safe by quoting alone here.
_IDENTIFIER = re.compile(r"^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_$]*$")

# Connector timeouts (seconds): fail fast rather than hang the request thread.
_LOGIN_TIMEOUT = 10
_NETWORK_TIMEOUT = 30


class ProfileUnsupportedError(DataQError):
    status_code = 422
    code = "profile_unsupported"


class ProfileTargetInvalidError(DataQError):
    status_code = 422
    code = "profile_target_invalid"


class ProfileIdentifierInvalidError(DataQError):
    status_code = 422
    code = "profile_identifier_invalid"


class ProfileColumnNotFoundError(DataQError):
    status_code = 422
    code = "profile_column_not_found"


class ProfileFailedError(DataQError):
    status_code = 502
    code = "profile_failed"


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ColumnProfile:
    column: str
    null_count: int
    null_fraction: float
    distinct_count: int | None  # None when the column's values aren't hashable
    min_value: Any
    max_value: Any
    top_values: list[dict[str, Any]]  # [{"value": ..., "count": int}]


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ProfileResult:
    """A profiled target. Identity fields are type-specific: SQL datasources set
    `table` / `schema`, flat-file datasources set `path` / `file_format`."""

    row_count: int
    columns: list[ColumnProfile]
    table: str | None = None
    schema: str | None = None
    catalog: str | None = None
    path: str | None = None
    file_format: str | None = None


# ───────────────────────── shared stat contract ────────────────────


def null_fraction(null_count: int, row_count: int) -> float:
    """Fraction of rows that are null, guarding the empty-target divide-by-zero.

    The one stat definition the SQL profiler (`assemble_profile`) and the pandas
    profiler (`profile_dataframe`) can actually share — both must agree that a
    0-row target reports `0.0`, not `1.0` or a `ZeroDivisionError` (#147). The
    other contract points (distinct excludes nulls; top values are non-null,
    highest-count-first) are structurally SQL-vs-pandas and can't share code, so
    they're pinned by the parallel-path tests instead.
    """
    return (null_count / row_count) if row_count else 0.0


# ───────────────────────── pure query builders ─────────────────────


def validate_identifier(name: str | None) -> str:
    """Validate `name` against the plain-identifier allowlist and return it.

    Raises `ProfileIdentifierInvalidError` (422) for anything that isn't a plain
    identifier. The SQLAlchemy Core builders quote safely on their own; this is
    defence-in-depth and turns an odd name into a clean 422 instead of a quoted
    column that simply doesn't exist.
    """
    if not name or not _IDENTIFIER.match(name):
        raise ProfileIdentifierInvalidError(
            "not a valid table/schema/column identifier", detail={"identifier": name}
        )
    return name


def _table(schema: str, table_name: str, catalog: str | None = None) -> Any:
    """A Core table clause, optionally with a 3-level namespace (Unity Catalog).

    With a `catalog`, the namespace is `catalog.schema` passed as an unquoted
    `quoted_name` so the dialect emits three dotted parts (`catalog.schema.table`)
    rather than quoting the dotted string as one identifier. Safe because every
    part is allowlist-validated.
    """
    validate_identifier(schema)
    if catalog is not None:
        validate_identifier(catalog)
        namespace: Any = quoted_name(f"{catalog}.{schema}", quote=False)
    else:
        namespace = schema
    return table(validate_identifier(table_name), schema=namespace)

    def run_monitors(
        self, *, table: str, schema: str | None, monitors: list[MonitorSpec]
    ) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
        """Evaluate freshness/volume monitors via scalar SQL aggregates (no GX).

        Opens one SQLAlchemy connection over the same DSN the GX path uses; Snowflake
        addresses the target as ``schema.table`` (the database is in the DSN), so no
        catalog. A connection-level failure (can't reach the warehouse) propagates,
        failing the run like the GX path; a bad monitor errors only itself."""
        from sqlalchemy import create_engine, text

        engine = create_engine(self._connection_string, connect_args=self._connect_args or {})
        try:
            with engine.connect() as conn:
                return evaluate_monitors(
                    lambda sql: conn.execute(text(sql)).scalar(),
                    table=table,
                    schema=schema or self._config.schema_,
                    catalog=None,
                    monitors=monitors,
                )
        finally:
            engine.dispose()


def build_snowflake_runner(
    *,
    config: dict[str, Any],
    secret_ref: str | None,
    secret_store: SecretStore,
) -> SnowflakeCheckRunner:

input.content:
"""Shared SQL-datasource primitives (#428).

The single source of truth for two things every SQL-speaking path needs and
previously copied per-module:

* :data:`SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE` / :func:`is_sql_identifier` — the plain-identifier
  allowlist (the Snowflake/Databricks unquoted-identifier set: letter/underscore
  lead, then word chars or ``$``). SQL identifiers have no bind-parameter slot,
  so every path that interpolates a user-supplied table/schema/column into a
  query string (monitor config, the profiler's query builders) must validate
  against this exact set first. One definition means widening it (e.g. quoted /
  unicode identifiers) happens everywhere at once, never in one copy silently.
  Callers keep their own error types (a monitor raises ``MonitorConfigError``,
  the profiler a 422 ``ProfileIdentifierInvalidError``) — the shared piece is
  the *decision*, not the failure shape.

* :func:`run_monitors_over_engine` — the one engine → connection → scalar loop
  the SQL runners (Snowflake / Unity Catalog) execute monitor checks through.
  The runners differ only in how they build their URL/engine; the lifecycle
  (open one connection, feed ``evaluate_monitors`` a scalar fetcher, let a
  connection failure propagate to fail the run) is identical and lives here.
  The caller owns the engine (build + dispose) — that seam is what lets #427
  later thread a per-run shared engine through without touching this helper.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import re
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Final

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from sqlalchemy.engine import Engine

    from backend.app.datasources.base import CheckOutcome, MonitorSpec

SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE: Final = re.compile(r"^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_$]*$")


def is_sql_identifier(name: object) -> bool:
    """True when ``name`` is a plain SQL identifier safe to interpolate.

    Anything else (spaces, quotes, dots, non-strings) is refused — it can't be
    made injection-safe by quoting alone at the call sites that use this.
    """
    return isinstance(name, str) and bool(SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE.match(name))


def run_monitors_over_engine(
    engine: Engine,
    *,
    table: str,
    schema: str | None,
    catalog: str | None,
    monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
    """Run monitor checks over ONE connection from ``engine``, one outcome each.

    Opens a single connection and sources every monitor's scalar from it via
    ``evaluate_monitors`` (a bad monitor errors only itself; a connection-level
    failure propagates and fails the whole run — the open happens before the
    per-monitor loop). The engine's lifecycle belongs to the caller.
    """
    # Lazy imports: sqlalchemy is heavy (runner-module convention), and monitors.py
    # imports this module for the identifier allowlist — importing it back at
    # module scope would be a cycle.
    from sqlalchemy import text

    from backend.app.datasources.monitors import evaluate_monitors

    with engine.connect() as conn:
        return evaluate_monitors(
            lambda sql: conn.execute(text(sql)).scalar(),
            table=table,
            schema=schema,
            catalog=catalog,
            monitors=monitors,
        )

# SQL identifier we're willing to interpolate into the aggregate. Monitor config is

_IDENT_RE = re.compile(r"^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_$]*$")


class MonitorConfigError(ValueError):
    """A monitor check's config is missing/invalid (bad column, range, or kind)."""


def _ident(name: object, *, what: str) -> str:
    """Validate a SQL identifier (so it's safe to interpolate) and return it."""
    if not isinstance(name, str) or not _IDENT_RE.match(name):
        raise MonitorConfigError(f"invalid {what} identifier: {name!r}")
    return name
input.new_string:
class MonitorConfigError(ValueError):
    """A monitor check's config is missing/invalid (bad column, range, or kind)."""


def _ident(name: object, *, what: str) -> str:
    """Validate a SQL identifier (so it's safe to interpolate) and return it.

    Monitor config is user-authored, so the column/table/schema must be validated
    before they touch a query string (no bound-param slot for an identifier). The
    allowlist itself is the shared `datasources.sql` one (#428) — one source of
    truth with the profiler's validator."""
    if not is_sql_identifier(name):
        raise MonitorConfigError(f"invalid {what} identifier: {name!r}")
    assert isinstance(name, str)  # narrowed by is_sql_identifier  # noqa: S101
    return name

"""A monitor check's config is missing/invalid (bad column, range, or kind)."""


def _ident(name: object, *, what: str) -> str:
    """Validate a SQL identifier (so it's safe to interpolate) and return it.

    Monitor config is user-authored, so the column/table/schema must be validated
    before they touch a query string (no bound-param slot for an identifier). The
    allowlist itself is the shared `datasources.sql` one (#428) — one source of
    truth with the profiler's validator."""
    if not is_sql_identifier(name):
        raise MonitorConfigError(f"invalid {what} identifier: {name!r}")
    assert isinstance(name, str)  # narrowed by is_sql_identifier
    return name


def qualified_table(*, table: str, schema: str | None, catalog: str | None) -> str:
    """A dotted, identifier-validated ``[catalog.][schema.]table`` for a monitor query.

    A ``catalog`` with no ``schema`` is rejected: skipping the None ``schema`` would

attachment.snippet:
1	"""Column profiler — per-column statistics for the check editor.
2	
3	Given a target (a SQL table or a flat file) and a set of columns on a suite's
4	connection, compute the stats an author needs before writing expectations: row
5	count, null count / fraction, distinct count, min / max, and the most frequent
6	values. Persists nothing — a read-only authoring aid (the check-editor "profile
7	on table/file select" panel).
8	
9	`profile_connection` dispatches on the connection type:
10	
11	* **SQL datasources** (Snowflake + Unity Catalog) — aggregate the stats
12	  in-warehouse with one round-trip + a top-values query per column, via the
13	  datasource's SQLAlchemy dialect. Unity Catalog adds a `catalog` so the table is
14	  qualified `catalog.schema.table` (3-level namespace); Snowflake is `schema.table`.
15	* **Flat-file datasources** (ADLS Gen2, S3) — download a *sample* of the file
16	  (`_SAMPLE_ROWS` rows) into Pandas and compute the same stats locally. CSV and
17	  Parquet are supported; stats are therefore over the sample, not the whole file.
18	
19	**SQL-injection safety.** For SQL datasources, table / schema / column names are
20	caller-supplied and become SQL *identifiers* (they can't be bound parameters).
21	Queries are built with the SQLAlchemy Core expression language (`select` /
22	`table` / `column`) — never string formatting — so the dialect does the quoting
23	and there is no raw-SQL sink. As defence-in-depth (and a clean early 422) each
24	identifier is also validated against a strict allowlist. Flat-file columns are
25	checked for existence against the loaded frame instead (a missing column is a
26	clean 422, and Pandas indexing never builds SQL).
27	
28	Like the GX adapter, the pure pieces (identifier validation, query building,
29	dataframe profiling, result assembly) are unit-testable without a live
30	datasource; the I/O seams (`_open_connection`, `_read_dataframe`) are
31	monkeypatched in tests, and a live smoke is deferred.
32	"""
33	
34	from __future__ import annotations
35	
36	import io
37	import math
38	from collections.abc import Callable, Generator, Mapping
39	from contextlib import contextmanager
40	from dataclasses import dataclass
41	from typing import Any
42	
43	from sqlalchemy import column, distinct, func, literal_column, quoted_name, select, table
44	from sqlalchemy.sql import Select
45	
46	from backend.app.core.errors import DataQError
47	from backend.app.core.jsonsafe import sanitize_json
48	from backend.app.core.logging import get_logger
49	from backend.app.core.secrets import SecretStore
50	from backend.app.datasources.flatfile import download_bytes, format_from_path
51	from backend.app.datasources.sql import is_sql_identifier
52	from backend.app.datasources.iceberg import (
53	    IcebergConfig,
54	    iceberg_credentials,
55	    load_iceberg_table,
56	    read_iceberg_dataframe,
57	)
58	from backend.app.datasources.iceberg import list_iceberg_columns as iceberg_column_names
59	from backend.app.datasources.snowflake import (
60	    SnowflakeConfig,
61	    build_connect_args,
62	    build_connection_string,
63	)
64	from backend.app.datasources.unity_catalog import UnityCatalogConfig, build_databricks_url
65	from backend.app.db.models import Connection
66	from backend.app.services.column_classification import ColumnClass, classify_column
67	
68	log = get_logger(__name__)
69	
70	# Formats the profiler can actually parse. NOT redundant with
71	# flatfile.format_from_path (which only recognises path extensions): this also
72	# validates the caller's `explicit` file_format override (an arbitrary string),
73	# and is deliberately a *subset* of recognised formats — a format can be
74	# recognised by path yet unsupported here, which should still 422 (#147).
75	_SUPPORTED_FORMATS = {"csv", "parquet"}
76	# Flat-file profiling reads at most this many rows — stats are over the sample.
77	_SAMPLE_ROWS = 100_000
78	
79	
80	# Connector timeouts (seconds): fail fast rather than hang the request thread.
81	_LOGIN_TIMEOUT = 10
82	_NETWORK_TIMEOUT = 30
83	
84	
85	class ProfileUnsupportedError(DataQError):
86	    status_code = 422
87	    code = "profile_unsupported"
88	
89	
90	class ProfileTargetInvalidError(DataQError):
91	    status_code = 422
92	    code = "profile_target_invalid"
93	
94	
95	class ProfileIdentifierInvalidError(DataQError):
96	    status_code = 422
97	    code = "profile_identifier_invalid"
98	
99	
100	class ProfileColumnNotFoundError(DataQError):
101	    status_code = 422
102	    code = "profile_column_not_found"
103	
104	
105	class ProfileFailedError(DataQError):
106	    status_code = 502
107	    code = "profile_failed"
108	
109	
110	@dataclass(frozen=True)
111	class ColumnProfile:
112	    column: str
113	    null_count: int
114	    null_fraction: float
115	    distinct_count: int | None  # None when the column's values aren't hashable
116	    min_value: Any
117	    max_value: Any
118	    top_values: list[dict[str, Any]]  # [{"value": ..., "count": int}]
119	
120	
121	@dataclass(frozen=True)
122	class ProfileResult:
123	    """A profiled target. Identity fields are type-specific: SQL datasources set
124	    `table` / `schema`, flat-file datasources set `path` / `file_format`."""
125	
126	    row_count: int
127	    columns: list[ColumnProfile]
128	    table: str | None = None
129	    schema: str | None = None
130	    catalog: str | None = None
131	    path: str | None = None
132	    file_format: str | None = None
133	
134	
135	# ───────────────────────── shared stat contract ────────────────────
136	
137	
138	def null_fraction(null_count: int, row_count: int) -> float:
139	    """Fraction of rows that are null, guarding the empty-target divide-by-zero.
140	
141	    The one stat definition the SQL profiler (`assemble_profile`) and the pandas
142	    profiler (`profile_dataframe`) can actually share — both must agree that a
143	    0-row target reports `0.0`, not `1.0` or a `ZeroDivisionError` (#147). The
144	    other contract points (distinct excludes nulls; top values are non-null,
145	    highest-count-first) are structurally SQL-vs-pandas and can't share code, so
146	    they're pinned by the parallel-path tests instead.
147	    """
148	    return (null_count / row_count) if row_count else 0.0
149	
150	
151	# ───────────────────────── pure query builders ─────────────────────
152	
153	
154	def validate_identifier(name: str | None) -> str:
155	    """Validate `name` against the plain-identifier allowlist and return it.
156	
157	    Raises `ProfileIdentifierInvalidError` (422) for anything that isn't a plain
158	    identifier per the shared `datasources.sql` allowlist (#428 — one source of
159	    truth with the monitor engine's validator). The SQLAlchemy Core builders
160	    quote safely on their own; this is defence-in-depth and turns an odd name
161	    into a clean 422 instead of a quoted column that simply doesn't exist.
162	    """
163	    if name is None or not is_sql_identifier(name):
164	        raise ProfileIdentifierInvalidError(
165	            "not a valid table/schema/column identifier", detail={"identifier": name}
166	        )
167	    return name
168	
169	
170	def _table(schema: str, table_name: str, catalog: str | None = None) -> Any:
171	    """A Core table clause, optionally with a 3-level namespace (Unity Catalog).
172	
173	    With a `catalog`, the namespace is `catalog.schema` passed as an unquoted
174	    `quoted_name` so the dialect emits three dotted parts (`catalog.schema.table`)
175	    rather than quoting the dotted string as one identifier. Safe because every
176	    part is allowlist-validated.
177	    """
178	    validate_identifier(schema)
179	    if catalog is not None:
180	        validate_identifier(catalog)
181	        namespace: Any = quoted_name(f"{catalog}.{schema}", quote=False)
182	    else:
183	        namespace = schema
184	    return table(validate_identifier(table_name), schema=namespace)
185

def run_monitors(
        self, *, table: str, schema: str | None, monitors: list[MonitorSpec]
    ) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
        """Evaluate freshness/volume monitors via scalar SQL aggregates (no GX).

        Opens one SQLAlchemy connection over the same DSN the GX path uses; Snowflake
        addresses the target as ``schema.table`` (the database is in the DSN), so no
        catalog. A connection-level failure (can't reach the warehouse) propagates,
        failing the run like the GX path; a bad monitor errors only itself."""
        from sqlalchemy import create_engine, text

        engine = create_engine(self._connection_string, connect_args=self._connect_args or {})
        try:
            with engine.connect() as conn:
                return evaluate_monitors(
                    lambda sql: conn.execute(text(sql)).scalar(),
                    table=table,
                    schema=schema or self._config.schema_,
                    catalog=None,
                    monitors=monitors,
                )
        finally:
            engine.dispose()

def run_monitors(
        self, *, table: str, schema: str | None, monitors: list[MonitorSpec]
    ) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
        """Evaluate freshness/volume monitors via scalar SQL aggregates over the SQL
        Warehouse (no GX / no DataFrame read). The pinned ``catalog`` qualifies the
        target as ``catalog.schema.table``. A connection failure propagates; a bad
        monitor errors only itself."""
        from sqlalchemy import create_engine, text

        engine = create_engine(
            build_databricks_url(self._config, self._token, catalog=self._catalog)
        )
        try:
            with engine.connect() as conn:
                return evaluate_monitors(
                    lambda sql: conn.execute(text(sql)).scalar(),
                    table=table,
                    schema=schema,
                    catalog=self._catalog,
                    monitors=monitors,
                )
        finally:
            engine.dispose()

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Unity Catalog (Databricks) connection adapter.

A datasource (CLAUDE.md §4): DQ checks run against Unity Catalog tables via a
Databricks SQL Warehouse. Week 2 ships only the `ConnectionAdapter` seam (config
validation + connectivity `test`).

**Runner seam note:** the *check-run* path for UC must sit behind a
``UnityCatalogCheckRunner`` interface so v1.1 can swap GX for Databricks Labs DQX
on DLT/streaming (CLAUDE.md §5, ADR 0003). That runner is Week-3 work and is
deliberately **not** built here — this module is connection config + a
connectivity probe only.

Auth is a **personal access token (PAT)** — the v1 default, held in the
SecretStore (no credential-less mode, so none of the ADLS/S3 ``secret_ref``
nullability deferral applies). ``test`` opens a SQL-Warehouse connection and runs
``SELECT 1`` — a green test means the workspace + warehouse are reachable and the
PAT authenticates. ``databricks-sql-connector`` is imported lazily (per
``core/secrets.py``); like the other adapters it runs live and fails-soft pending
real credentials.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Any
from urllib.parse import quote_plus, urlparse

import great_expectations as gx
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, field_validator

from backend.app.core.secrets import SecretStore
from backend.app.datasources.base import CheckOutcome, CheckSpec, MonitorSpec, SuiteOutcome
from backend.app.datasources.gx_runner import run_expectations
from backend.app.datasources.sql import run_monitors_over_engine


class UnityCatalogConfig(BaseModel):
    """Non-secret Databricks/UC connection config (the PAT comes from secrets).

    Maps from ``Connection.config``. ``workspace_url`` is the workspace root
    (e.g. ``https://adb-1234.5.azuredatabricks.net``); ``warehouse_id`` is the
    SQL Warehouse id, from which the connector's ``http_path`` is built. The PAT
    is resolved from the SecretStore at test time.
    """

    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

    workspace_url: str
    warehouse_id: str

    @field_validator("workspace_url")
    @classmethod
    def _http_url(cls, value: str) -> str:
        if not value.startswith(("http://", "https://")):
            raise ValueError("workspace_url must start with http:// or https://")
        return value.rstrip("/")

    @property
    def server_hostname(self) -> str:
        return urlparse(self.workspace_url).netloc

    @property
    def http_path(self) -> str:
        return f"/sql/1.0/warehouses/{self.warehouse_id}"


class UnityCatalogConnectionAdapter:
    """`ConnectionAdapter` for Unity Catalog — config validation + a SELECT 1 probe."""

    def validate_config(self, raw: dict[str, Any]) -> UnityCatalogConfig:
        return UnityCatalogConfig.model_validate(raw)

    def test(self, raw: dict[str, Any], secret: str, **_: Any) -> None:
        """Open a SQL-Warehouse connection and run ``SELECT 1``; raise on failure.

        ``secret`` is the Databricks PAT. Deliberately GX/DQX-free — a lightweight
        connectivity probe, not a suite run.
        """
        from databricks import sql

        config = self.validate_config(raw)
        # databricks-sql-connector is only partially typed; treat the connection
        # as dynamic so strict mypy doesn't flag no-untyped-call on its methods.
        connection: Any = sql.connect(
            server_hostname=config.server_hostname,
            http_path=config.http_path,
            access_token=secret,
        )
        try:
            cursor = connection.cursor()
            try:
                cursor.execute("SELECT 1")
                cursor.fetchone()
            finally:
                cursor.close()
        finally:
            connection.close()


def build_databricks_url(
    config: UnityCatalogConfig, token: str, *, catalog: str | None = None
) -> str:
    """SQLAlchemy URL for the Databricks SQL Warehouse (databricks dialect).

    The PAT, http_path and `catalog` are URL-encoded. Pinning `catalog` sets the
    session default so a 2-level `schema.table` reference resolves to
    `catalog.schema.table`; the profiler leaves it unset and qualifies the
    namespace in the query instead.
    """
    url = (
        f"REDACTED"
        f"?http_path={quote_plus(config.http_path)}"
    )
    if catalog:
        url += f"&catalog={quote_plus(catalog)}"
    return url


class UnityCatalogCheckRunner:
    """GX `CheckRunner` for Unity Catalog via the Databricks SQL Warehouse.

    The UC run path reads the target table into a pandas DataFrame and validates
    that frame with GX — the "GX DataFrame datasource" shape (CLAUDE.md §5), the
    same shape Databricks Labs DQX consumes, so v1.1 can swap GX for DQX behind
    this same interface without touching the suite/check/result layer.

    `table` + `schema` come from `run_checks` (the suite's target); `catalog` is
    fixed per run (held here). Reflecting + reading the table is the live seam
    (`_read_table`), monkeypatched in tests; GX then runs in-process on the
    returned frame, so the validation path itself is fully covered.
    """

    def __init__(self, *, config: UnityCatalogConfig, token: str, catalog: str) -> None:
        self._config = config
        self._token = token
        self._catalog = catalog

    def _read_table(self, *, table: str, schema: str | None) -> Any:
        """Reflect + read the whole table into a DataFrame (live seam).

        `read_sql_table` reflects through SQLAlchemy (proper dialect quoting), so
        the table/schema identifiers are never string-formatted into SQL; the
        pinned catalog + `schema` qualify it to `catalog.schema.table`.
        """
        import pandas as pd
        from sqlalchemy import create_engine

        engine = create_engine(
            build_databricks_url(self._config, self._token, catalog=self._catalog)
        )
        try:
            return pd.read_sql_table(table, engine, schema=schema)
        finally:
            engine.dispose()

    def run_checks(
        self,
        *,
        table: str,
        schema: str | None,
        checks: list[CheckSpec],
        index_columns: list[str] | None = None,
    ) -> SuiteOutcome:
        df = self._read_table(table=table, schema=schema)
        context = gx.get_context(mode="ephemeral")
        asset = context.data_sources.add_pandas(name="uc").add_dataframe_asset(name="table")
        batch_definition = asset.add_batch_definition_whole_dataframe(name="whole_dataframe")
        return run_expectations(
            context,
            batch_definition=batch_definition,
            checks=checks,
            name="suite-uc",
            batch_parameters={"dataframe": df},
            index_columns=index_columns,
        )

    def run_monitors(
        self, *, table: str, schema: str | None, monitors: list[MonitorSpec]
    ) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
        """Evaluate freshness/volume monitors via scalar SQL aggregates over the SQL
        Warehouse (no GX / no DataFrame read). The pinned ``catalog`` qualifies the
        target as ``catalog.schema.table``. A connection failure propagates; a bad
        monitor errors only itself."""
        from sqlalchemy import create_engine, text

        engine = create_engine(
            build_databricks_url(self._config, self._token, catalog=self._catalog)
        )
        try:
            with engine.connect() as conn:
                return evaluate_monitors(
                    lambda sql: conn.execute(text(sql)).scalar(),
                    table=table,
                    schema=schema,
                    catalog=self._catalog,
                    monitors=monitors,
                )
        finally:
            engine.dispose()


def build_unity_catalog_runner(
    *, config: dict[str, Any], secret_ref: str | None, secret_store: SecretStore, catalog: str
) -> UnityCatalogCheckRunner:
    """Build a runner from a UC `Connection`'s primitives + the target `catalog`.

    Mirrors `build_snowflake_runner`: resolves the PAT eagerly and takes the raw
    config dict (not the ORM model) to keep the adapter decoupled from `db/`.
    """
    if not secret_ref:
        raise ValueError("Unity Catalog connection requires secret_ref for the PAT")
    uc_config = UnityCatalogConfig.model_validate(config)
    token = secret_store.get(secret_ref)
    return UnityCatalogCheckRunner(config=uc_config, token=token, catalog=catalog)

"""Tests for the shared SQL-datasource primitives (#428) — the single-source
identifier allowlist and the deduped monitor-over-engine execution loop."""

from __future__ import annotations

import pytest
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, text

from backend.app.datasources.base import MonitorSpec
from backend.app.datasources.sql import is_sql_identifier, run_monitors_over_engine

# ───────────────────────── identifier allowlist ─────────────────────────


@pytest.mark.parametrize("name", ["orders", "_private", "COL$1", "a1_b2", "T"])
def test_valid_identifiers_pass(name: str) -> None:
    assert is_sql_identifier(name)


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "name",
    ["", "1abc", "a b", 'a"b', "a;drop table t", "a.b", "col-name", None, 42, ["orders"]],
)
def test_invalid_identifiers_and_non_strings_fail(name: object) -> None:
    assert not is_sql_identifier(name)


def test_allowlist_is_shared_by_monitors_and_profiler() -> None:
    # The point of #428: one source of truth. Both consumers must reject through
    # the same decision — pin that they actually route through it.
    from backend.app.datasources.monitors import MonitorConfigError, _ident
    from backend.app.services.profile_service import (
        ProfileIdentifierInvalidError,
        validate_identifier,
    )

    with pytest.raises(MonitorConfigError):
        _ident("a;drop", what="column")
    with pytest.raises(ProfileIdentifierInvalidError):
        validate_identifier("a;drop")
    assert _ident("fine_col", what="column") == "fine_col"
    assert validate_identifier("fine_col") == "fine_col"


# ───────────────────────── run_monitors_over_engine ─────────────────────────


@pytest.fixture
def engine():  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    eng = create_engine("sqlite://")
    with eng.begin() as conn:
        conn.execute(text("CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER)"))
        conn.execute(text("INSERT INTO orders (id) VALUES (1), (2), (3)"))
    yield eng
    eng.dispose()


def test_volume_monitor_runs_over_one_engine_connection(engine) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    outcomes = run_monitors_over_engine(
        engine,
        table="orders",
        schema=None,
        catalog=None,
        monitors=[MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 1, "max_rows": 10})],
    )
    assert len(outcomes) == 1
    assert outcomes[0].success
    assert outcomes[0].metric_value == 0.0
    assert outcomes[0].observed_value == {"row_count": 3, "deviation_pct": 0.0}


def test_bad_monitor_errors_only_itself(engine) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    # First monitor queries a nonexistent column (SQL error at fetch time); the
    # sibling volume monitor on the same connection must still produce a result.
    outcomes = run_monitors_over_engine(
        engine,
        table="orders",
        schema=None,
        catalog=None,
        monitors=[
            MonitorSpec(kind="freshness", config={"column": "no_such_col"}),
            MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 1, "max_rows": 10}),
        ],
    )
    assert [o.errored for o in outcomes] == [True, False]
    assert outcomes[1].success


def test_connection_failure_propagates(tmp_path) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    # A datasource-establishment failure must fail the whole run (raise), never
    # degrade into per-monitor errors — the connect happens before the loop.
    eng = create_engine(f"sqlite:///{tmp_path}/no_such_dir/db.sqlite")
    with pytest.raises(Exception, match="unable to open database file"):

toolUseResult.content:
"""Tests for the shared SQL-datasource primitives (#428) — the single-source
identifier allowlist and the deduped monitor-over-engine execution loop."""

from __future__ import annotations

import pytest
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, text

from backend.app.datasources.base import MonitorSpec
from backend.app.datasources.sql import is_sql_identifier, run_monitors_over_engine

# ───────────────────────── identifier allowlist ─────────────────────────


@pytest.mark.parametrize("name", ["orders", "_private", "COL$1", "a1_b2", "T"])
def test_valid_identifiers_pass(name: str) -> None:
    assert is_sql_identifier(name)


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "name",
    ["", "1abc", "a b", 'a"b', "a;drop table t", "a.b", "col-name", None, 42, ["orders"]],
)
def test_invalid_identifiers_and_non_strings_fail(name: object) -> None:
    assert not is_sql_identifier(name)


def test_allowlist_is_shared_by_monitors_and_profiler() -> None:
    # The point of #428: one source of truth. Both consumers must reject through
    # the same decision — pin that they actually route through it.
    from backend.app.datasources.monitors import MonitorConfigError, _ident
    from backend.app.services.profile_service import (
        ProfileIdentifierInvalidError,
        validate_identifier,
    )

    with pytest.raises(MonitorConfigError):
        _ident("a;drop", what="column")
    with pytest.raises(ProfileIdentifierInvalidError):
        validate_identifier("a;drop")
    assert _ident("fine_col", what="column") == "fine_col"
    assert validate_identifier("fine_col") == "fine_col"


# ───────────────────────── run_monitors_over_engine ─────────────────────────


@pytest.fixture
def engine():  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    eng = create_engine("sqlite://")
    with eng.begin() as conn:
        conn.execute(text("CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER)"))
        conn.execute(text("INSERT INTO orders (id) VALUES (1), (2), (3)"))
    yield eng
    eng.dispose()


def test_volume_monitor_runs_over_one_engine_connection(engine) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    outcomes = run_monitors_over_engine(
        engine,
        table="orders",
        schema=None,
        catalog=None,
        monitors=[MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 1, "max_rows": 10})],
    )
    assert len(outcomes) == 1
    assert outcomes[0].success
    assert outcomes[0].metric_value == 0.0
    assert outcomes[0].observed_value == {"row_count": 3, "deviation_pct": 0.0}


def test_bad_monitor_errors_only_itself(engine) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    # First monitor queries a nonexistent column (SQL error at fetch time); the
    # sibling volume monitor on the same connection must still produce a result.
    outcomes = run_monitors_over_engine(
        engine,
        table="orders",
        schema=None,
        catalog=None,
        monitors=[
            MonitorSpec(kind="freshness", config={"column": "no_such_col"}),
            MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 1, "max_rows": 10}),
        ],
    )
    assert [o.errored for o in outcomes] == [True, False]
    assert outcomes[1].success


def test_connection_failure_propagates(tmp_path) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    # A datasource-establishment failure must fail the whole run (raise), never
    # degrade into per-monitor errors — the connect happens before the loop.
    eng = create_engine(f"sqlite:///{tmp_path}/no_such_dir/db.sqlite")
    with pytest.raises(Exception, match="unable to open database file"):
        run_monitors_over_engine(
            eng,
            table="orders",
            schema=None,
            catalog=None,
            monitors=[MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 0, "max_rows": 1})],
        )

(**2026-07-13 — prod lineage restored + the #829/#830 pair merged.** The lineage outage was ours: the dbt connection's ADLS SAS **expired 6 Jul**, so every 10-min poll failed `ClientAuthenticationError` and DataQ never read `manifest.json` — the dbt builds stayed green and kept publishing artifacts nobody consumed. Marquez was a red herring; the live path is the #759 dbt-manifest one. Re-minted the SAS (1y), re-ran the build, and the chain fired end to end (poll → `refresh_dbt_lineage` → `lineage_edges` → graph; the trigger-binding also ran the bound suite 4/4 green). **Two systemic defects fall out, still open on [#828](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/828):** a failing poll has **no user-visible signal** and the UI's empty-lineage state is indistinguishable from "this asset has no upstreams"; and `_POLL_LOOKBACK` is 15 min, so once auth was fixed the poll succeeded and still recorded **nothing** — every build produced during the outage had aged out and is stranded in the artifact store, recoverable only by re-running the producer. [#823](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/823) narrowed to the still-unproven Marquez pull path. Shipped alongside: [#829](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/829) — a suite was **unshareable from a phone** (the Add button painted at x=407 on a 390px viewport) — and [#830](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/830) — the assets UI printed the raw OL namespace (an Iceberg DSN) instead of a human label. **Both agentic reviews earned their keep:** on #829 the review caught that the new e2e spec was **worthless** (`toBeInViewport()` defaults to `ratio: 0`, and 71% of the broken button was on screen — it passed on the bug); on #830 it caught a **missed fifth render site** (the dashboard) and that the label still **leaked the DB username** when a catalog URI had no path, which made the commit message's own claim false.) (**user follow-up batch #801–#805 all closed 2026-07-12** — the five UI/ops follow-ups filed off the ADR-0015 session: mobile overlay nav ([#817](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/817)), hierarchical asset browse ([#818](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/818)), asset-health split into connection-vs-data-quality axes ([#819](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/819)), the left-to-right lineage graph ([#820](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/820)), and the Marquez local verification + runbook correction ([#821](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/821)). Four latent bugs surfaced and were fixed en route: a run that evaluated nothing read as a green "Passing"; a `cancelled` run rolled up green on **both** health axes; an asset→asset navigation showed the *previous* asset's data (nothing could reach it before the graph's clickable nodes); and the demo seed had **no lineage at all**, so a fresh install could not show the feature. **#813 closed via [#814](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/814)** — weekly `/security-scan` OWASP spot-check found the MCP `create_check`/`update_check` tools had no Pydantic layer, so `name`/`expectation_type` could exceed the REST 256/128 bounds and hit a raw Postgres `StringDataRightTruncation` instead of a clean 422; pushed `check_service.validate_lengths` (now public) into both, extended to `suite_io_service.import_suite`'s direct `Check(...)` construction too (agentic review caught that same-class gap pre-merge). Same session: 13 open CodeQL code-scanning alerts triaged — 10 dismissed as false-positive/by-design (module-level cache globals, a documented lazy-import cycle, an intentional test canary), 3 genuine (`side-effect-in-assert` ×2, `unnecessary-lambda`) fixed in [#815](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/815). **#799 comparison:columns + tolerance + optimization shipped (this PR)** — FDC's column grain as `comparison:columns` (per-column matched/mismatched/additional-per-side VALUE buckets, dropna parity, same sample shape → redaction/report reuse), numeric `config.tolerance` {absolute, relative} on both grains (the #808-review float32 finding — one-sided NULLs never tolerance-equal), and the shared keys+positions alignment pass (×1.52 on the 1M-row benchmark, bucket parity asserted; integer keys now render "1" not "1.0" in samples); catalog fixture regenerated, per-column breakdown + tolerance fields in the UI. · **ADR [0015](adr/0015-two-connection-comparison-check-model.md) written + merged ([#790](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/790), 2026-07-12)** — the two-connection comparison check model: the suite stays single-connection and supplies the **target under test**; a `comparison` check adds one **source** ref. Closes the design gate reserved since ADR 0014; build set [#791](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/791)–[#795](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/795) filed on W3. **#795 comparison UI + report download shipped (this PR) — the ADR 0015 build is COMPLETE (#791–#795, five PRs in one session 2026-07-12)**: side-by-side check editor (source pane = connection picker + table-or-SQL per type + keys; target pane locked to the suite's connection — the §1 invariant made visible; common options below), RunDetail bucket counts + redacted sample tables + on-demand CSV/XLSX report download (derived server-side from the redacted buckets via new `comparison_report.py` + `openpyxl` (MIT, pinned), never stored), comparison-safe CheckEdit (name+thresholds only), catalog entry + regenerated GX-contract fixture, feature-matrix row; Playwright comparison spec green against the live local stack (which also re-validated #797 — the stale api container 429'd the suite until recreated). **#794 comparison run path shipped ([#809](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/809))** — `services/comparison_run.py` builds a per-run executor bound to the suite's resolved target (source side = the check's ref + `config.source` through `resolve_target`/batch materialization or a query spec; target side = the run's materialized table or `config.target_query`), reads both via the #792 reader, diffs with the #793 engine, and maps buckets → `CheckOutcome` (mismatch-% → `metric_value` under ADR 0016 banding; counts → `observed_value`; capped samples → `sample_failures`). `_run_outcomes` routes comparison checks to the injected executor (None → per-check `error`, callers stay safe); worker builds it only when the suite has comparison checks. Failure semantics #122: reader/engine refusals + unexpected exceptions → operational `error` results (classified messages, never raw text). The sample redactor gains a comparison branch — suffix-stripped policy matching so `pii_columns: [email]` masks `email_src`+`email_tgt`, unknown columns keep default-mask. **#793 comparison engine shipped ([#808](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/808))** — `datasources/comparison.py`, the FDC record-grain diff port (ADR 0014 §2: engine yes, app no): key-joined outer-merge buckets (matched / mismatched / additional-in-source / additional-in-target) with per-column mismatch counts, typed `DuplicateKeyError` (FDC silently drop_duplicates-ed — pairing must never be ambiguous) + `ComparisonInputError`, NA-safe nullable-`string` comparison (null==null matches; a literal "nan" ≠ NULL — fixes the FDC astype(str) quirk), pairwise numeric canonicalization (numpy NULL-int→float64 "10.0" vs Arrow "10" can't fabricate mismatches; dup-key detection on normalized keys, samples echo original values), mismatch-% badness scalar over the logical-row union, capped JSON-clean sample buckets. **#792 DatasetReader seam shipped ([#807](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/807))** — `services/dataset_reader.py`: per-datasource capped DataFrame reads behind profiler-style dict dispatch (SQL via the profiler engine plumbing, flat-file object read, `pyiceberg` scan; orchestration types 422), COUNT/scan-metadata preflight + post-read race guard + typed `DatasetTooLargeError` (fail-fast, never truncation), read-time read-only re-validation of query specs, `COMPARISON_MAX_ROWS` setting (default 100k, per-check `config.max_rows` override) documented in deploy/README. **#791 schema + authoring slice shipped ([#798](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/798))** — `checks.source_connection_id` FK + kind⇔presence CHECK (migration `b7c8d9e0f1a2`, up+down tested), author-time validation (source spec via `resolve_target`, read-only per-side SQL per ADR 0019, join keys, orchestration sources rejected), RESTRICT delete guard (409 `connection_in_use`), export/import `(name, env)` round-trip, version snapshots, and the run-path guard (a comparison check yields a per-check `error` result until the #794 runner — never fails its siblings' run). ## Carried over from v1

Everything that was still open, pending, or deferred in the v1 ledger at the tag. GitHub is
the source of truth for issue state; this register mirrors it so nothing carried is lost.

### Deferred v1 roadmap tasks (the 2 🔵 of 189)

| Item | Where tracked |
|---|---|

### Go-live follow-ups (filed 2026-07-03/04, open by choice — none blocking; all six since scheduled into the v1.1 cycle plan below)

| ✅ | [#849](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/849) **SECURITY: DataQ PATs were logged in PLAINTEXT to App Insights on every PAT-authenticated request.** Found live in prod telemetry while chasing an unrelated outage. `fastapi_azure_auth` logs the raw bearer when a JWT decode fails — and a PAT is never a valid JWT, so it failed *by construction*. Worse, `Security(azure_scheme)` is a FastAPI **dependency**: it resolves BEFORE `get_current_user`'s body, so the "PAT is tried first by prefix" ordering the code documents was **never actually first**. Every PAT request handed the credential to a JWT validator, which logged it in the clear, and only then fell through to the PAT path — which succeeded, so the request worked and nothing looked wrong. **A PAT is a live bearer credential**: anyone with App Insights read could authenticate as its owner, including the workspace-admin. Present since #613. **Both prod PATs revoked** (verified 401); re-minted only after the fix deployed. Fixed in two layers — the PAT never reaches the validator (`_PatAwareAzureScheme`), AND the logger scrubs bare credentials (`dq_live_…`/JWT/`Bearer …`) plus credential header keys. **Layer 2 is the architectural point: the call site that leaked was NOT OURS** — CLAUDE.md §10 already says redact at the logger, and grepping our own code for "places we log tokens" would never have found this, because we don't log it; a dependency does. (The MCP path had it right — it checks the prefix *before* calling the verifier.) Tests drive the REAL pipeline (a helper-only test stays green if the processor chain is reordered), mint a REAL token (a prefix literal would miss a changed alphabet), and intercept the LIBRARY's `__call__` (the buggy version also returned None — *after* logging — so asserting our return value would have passed on the bug); verified failing when redaction is removed. En route: I pasted the real token into the test file and betterleaks caught it in **git history** — a revoked credential is still a credential ([PR #851](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/851)) | 🔒 ops |
| ✅ | [#852](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/852) **The exporter was amplifying its own logs — 19,206 traces in 30 minutes (~10/sec).** `azure.core`'s HTTP policy logs a request AND response line for every SDK call, including the OTel exporter's own uploads to App Insights: upload → logged → reaches root → re-enters the export bridge → uploaded → logged again. The existing loop-breaker detached `opentelemetry` + `azure.monitor.opentelemetry` but **missed `azure.core`**, and unlike those, this loop doesn't need the backend to be down. The real cost isn't volume — **the noise drowned the signal**: while diagnosing a prod outage, a query for the app's own `orchestration_poll_completed` returned nothing usable, buried under thousands of `"Response status: 200"` lines. Observability that

[#823](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/823) narrowed to the still-unproven Marquez pull path. Shipped alongside: [#829](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/829) — a suite was **unshareable from a phone** (the Add button painted at x=407 on a 390px viewport) — and [#830](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/830) — the assets UI printed the raw OL namespace (an Iceberg DSN) instead of a human label. **Both agentic reviews earned their keep:** on #829 the review caught that the new e2e spec was **worthless** (`toBeInViewport()` defaults to `ratio: 0`, and 71% of the broken button was on screen — it passed on the bug); on #830 it caught a **missed fifth render site** (the dashboard) and that the label still **leaked the DB username** when a catalog URI had no path, which made the commit message's own claim false.) (**user follow-up batch #801–#805 all closed 2026-07-12** — the five UI/ops follow-ups filed off the ADR-0015 session: mobile overlay nav ([#817](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/817)), hierarchical asset browse ([#818](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/818)), asset-health split into connection-vs-data-quality axes ([#819](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/819)), the left-to-right lineage graph ([#820](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/820)), and the Marquez local verification + runbook correction ([#821](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/821)). Four latent bugs surfaced and were fixed en route: a run that evaluated nothing read as a green "Passing"; a `cancelled` run rolled up green on **both** health axes; an asset→asset navigation showed the *previous* asset's data (nothing could reach it before the graph's clickable nodes); and the demo seed had **no lineage at all**, so a fresh install could not show the feature. **#813 closed via [#814](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/814)** — weekly `/security-scan` OWASP spot-check found the MCP `create_check`/`update_check` tools had no Pydantic layer, so `name`/`expectation_type` could exceed the REST 256/128 bounds and hit a raw Postgres `StringDataRightTruncation` instead of a clean 422; pushed `check_service.validate_lengths` (now public) into both, extended to `suite_io_service.import_suite`'s direct `Check(...)` construction too (agentic review caught that same-class gap pre-merge). Same session: 13 open CodeQL code-scanning alerts triaged — 10 dismissed as false-positive/by-design (module-level cache globals, a documented lazy-import cycle, an intentional test canary), 3 genuine (`side-effect-in-assert` ×2, `unnecessary-lambda`) fixed in [#815](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/815). **#799 comparison:columns + tolerance + optimization shipped (this PR)** — FDC's column grain as `comparison:columns` (per-column matched/mismatched/additional-per-side VALUE buckets, dropna parity, same sample shape → redaction/report reuse), numeric `config.tolerance` {absolute, relative} on both grains (the #808-review float32 finding — one-sided NULLs never tolerance-equal), and the shared keys+positions alignment pass (×1.52 on the 1M-row benchmark, bucket parity asserted; integer keys now render "1" not "1.0" in samples); catalog fixture regenerated, per-column breakdown + tolerance fields in the UI. · **ADR [0015](adr/0015-two-connection-comparison-check-model.md) written + merged ([#790](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/790), 2026-07-12)** — the two-connection comparison check model: the suite stays single-connection and supplies the **target under test**; a `comparison` check adds one **source** ref. 350:4. Update the cycle subtotal and the **Snapshot** table (open PRs/issues).
| ✅ | [#849](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/849) **SECURITY: DataQ PATs were logged in PLAINTEXT to App Insights on every PAT-authenticated request.** Found live in prod telemetry while chasing an unrelated outage. `fastapi_azure_auth` logs the raw bearer when a JWT decode fails — and a PAT is never a valid JWT, so it failed *by construction*. Worse, `Security(azure_scheme)` is a FastAPI **dependency**: it resolves BEFORE `get_current_user`'s body, so the "PAT is tried first by prefix" ordering the code documents was **never actually first**. Every PAT request handed the credential to a JWT validator, which logged it in the clear, and only then fell through to the PAT path — which succeeded, so the request worked and nothing looked wrong. **A PAT is a live bearer credential**: anyone with App Insights read could authenticate as its owner, including the workspace-admin. Present since #613. **Both prod PATs revoked** (verified 401); re-minted only after the fix deployed. Fixed in two layers — the PAT never reaches the validator (`_PatAwareAzureScheme`), AND the logger scrubs bare credentials (`dq_live_…`/JWT/`Bearer …`) plus credential header keys. **Layer 2 is the architectural point: the call site that leaked was NOT OURS** — CLAUDE.md §10 already says redact at the logger, and grepping our own code for "places we log tokens" would never have found this, because we don't log it; a dependency does. (The MCP path had it right — it checks the prefix *before* calling the verifier.) Tests drive the REAL pipeline (a helper-only test stays green if the processor chain is reordered), mint a REAL token (a prefix literal would miss a changed alphabet), and intercept the LIBRARY's `__call__` (the buggy version also returned None — *after* logging — so asserting our return value would have passed on the bug); verified failing when redaction is removed. En route: I pasted the real token into the test file and betterleaks caught it in **git history** — a revoked credential is still a credential ([PR #851](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/851)) | 🔒 ops |
| ✅ | [#852](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/852) **The exporter was amplifying its own logs — 19,206 traces in 30 minutes (~10/sec).** `azure.core`'s HTTP policy logs a request AND response line for every SDK call, including the OTel exporter's own uploads to App Insights: upload → logged → reaches root → re-enters the export bridge → uploaded → logged again. The existing loop-breaker detached `opentelemetry` + `azure.monitor.opentelemetry` but **missed `azure.core`**, and unlike those, this loop doesn't need the backend to be down. The real cost isn't volume — **the noise drowned the signal**: while diagnosing a prod outage, a query for the app's own `orchestration_poll_completed` returned nothing usable, buried under thousands of `"Response status: 200"` lines. Observability that hides the event you're looking for is worse than none, because you trust it. Silenced at INFO (not detached — a genuine transport WARNING still gets through) ([PR #851](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/851)) | 🔒 ops |
| ✅ | [#845](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/845) **The lineage graph DEFEATED the no-leak 404** (found in prod: Olivia clicked a downstream mart → "asset not found"). The dead link was the symptom; the defect was that ADR 0034 decision 5 promises an asset outside your grants is 404-**no-leak**, and the endpoint is built so it cannot confirm such an asset exists — while the graph handed a non-grantee its **name, namespace, env and monitored-status** one click earlier. The guarantee was buying nothing. A neighbour behind the grant boundary is now **redacted, not omitted**: an anonymous node, drawn but not clickable, with a count ("1 connected asset is outside your access"). Dropping it would assert "nothing consumes this table" — false, and the same confident-empty-state lie #828/#823 were about; **we don't fix a leak by shipping a lie**. Redaction is server-side: a name hidden in CSS has still crossed the wire ([PR #848](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/848)) | 🔒 ops |
| ✅ | [#846](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/846) **Browse omitted suite-less assets — the list and the detail endpoint disagreed about what exists** (user: "under Snowflake ANALYTICS it shows 1 asset, there are at least 2"). The list filtered `Asset.id IN (SELECT suite.asset_id …)`, so a lineage-discovered table was unfindable **even for an admin**, though `get_visible_asset` explicitly hands admins those orphans. Pulling the thread showed the boundary was drawn in the wrong place: **a suite-less asset was treated as "outside your grants", but redaction protects a GRANT** — an asset nobody has granted is protected by nothing (no suites ⇒ no runs, results or samples; only its *name* was withheld, which lineage reveals anywa
