content.content: 27:| **Open issues** | **33** in the **`v1.1 Backlog`** milestone (76 open repo-wide) — `gh issue list --milestone 'v1.1 Backlog' --state open`; the population is stated here because the figure has silently drifted before, so **re-count it, don't decrement it**. (**2026-07-13 — prod lineage restored + the #829/#830 pair merged.** The lineage outage was ours: the dbt connection's ADLS SAS **expired 6 Jul**, so every 10-min poll failed `ClientAuthenticationError` and DataQ never read `manifest.json` — the dbt builds stayed green and kept publishing artifacts nobody consumed. Marquez was a red herring; the live path is the #759 dbt-manifest one. Re-minted the SAS (1y), re-ran the build, and the chain fired end to end (poll → `refresh_dbt_lineage` → `lineage_edges` → graph; the trigger-binding also ran the bound suite 4/4 green). **Two systemic defects fall out, still open on [#828](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/828):** a failing poll has **no user-visible signal** and the UI's empty-lineage state is indistinguishable from "this asset has no upstreams"; and `_POLL_LOOKBACK` is 15 min, so once auth was fixed the poll succeeded and still recorded **nothing** — every build produced during the outage had aged out and is stranded in the artifact store, recoverable only by re-running the producer. [#823](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/823) narrowed to the still-unproven Marquez pull path. Shipped alongside: [#829](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/829) — a suite was **unshareable from a phone** (the Add button painted at x=407 on a 390px viewport) — and [#830](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/830) — the assets UI printed the raw OL namespace (an Iceberg DSN) instead of a human label. **Both agentic reviews earned their keep:** on #829 the review caught that the new e2e spec was **worthless** (`toBeInViewport()` defaults to `ratio: 0`, and 71% of the broken button was on screen — it passed on the bug); on #830 it caught a **missed fifth render site** (the dashboard) and that the label still **leaked the DB username** when a catalog URI had no path, which made the commit message's own claim false.) (**user follow-up batch #801–#805 all closed 2026-07-12** — the five UI/ops follow-ups filed off the ADR-0015 session: mobile overlay nav ([#817](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/817)), hierarchical asset browse ([#818](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/818)), asset-health split into connection-vs-data-quality axes ([#819](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/819)), the left-to-right lineage graph ([#820](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/820)), and the Marquez local verification + runbook correction ([#821](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/821)). Four latent bugs surfaced and were fixed en route: a run that evaluated nothing read as a green "Passing"; a `cancelled` run rolled up green on **both** health axes; an asset→asset navigation showed the *previous* asset's data (nothing could reach it before the graph's clickable nodes); and the demo seed had **no lineage at all**, so a fresh install could not show the feature. **#813 closed via [#814](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/814)** — weekly `/security-scan` OWASP spot-check found the MCP `create_check`/`update_check` tools had no Pydantic layer, so `name`/`expectation_type` could exceed the REST 256/128 bounds and hit a raw Postgres `StringDataRightTruncation` instead of a clean 422; pushed `check_service.validate_lengths` (now public) into both, extended to `suite_io_service.import_suite`'s direct `Check(...)` construction too (agentic review caught that same-class gap pre-merge). Same session: 13 open CodeQL code-scanning alerts triaged — 10 dismissed as false-positive/by-design (module-level cache globals, a documented lazy-import cycle, an intentional test canary), 3 genuine (`side-effect-in-assert` ×2, `unnecessary-lambda`) fixed in [#815](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/815). **#799 comparison:columns + tolerance + optimization shipped (this PR)** — FDC's column grain as `comparison:columns` (per-column matched/mismatched/additional-per-side VALUE buckets, dropna parity, same sample shape → redaction/report reuse), numeric `config.tolerance` {absolute, relative} on both grains (the #808-review float32 finding — one-sided NULLs never tolerance-equal), and the shared keys+positions alignment pass (×1.52 on the 1M-row benchmark, bucket parity asserted; integer keys now render "1" not "1.0" in samples); catalog fixture regenerated, per-column breakdown + tolerance fields in the UI. · **ADR [0015](adr/0015-two-connection-comparison-check-model.md) written + merged ([#790](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/790), 2026-07-12)** — the two-connection comparison check model: the suite stays single-connection and supplies the **target under test**; a `comparison` check adds one **source** ref. Closes the design gate reserved since ADR 0014; build set [#791](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/791)–[#795](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/795) filed on W3. **#795 comparison UI + report download shipped (this PR) — the ADR 0015 build is COMPLETE (#791–#795, five PRs in one session 2026-07-12)**: side-by-side check editor (source pane = connection picker + table-or-SQL per type + keys; target pane locked to the suite's connection — the §1 invariant made visible; common options below), RunDetail bucket counts + redacted sample tables + on-demand CSV/XLSX report download (derived server-side from the redacted buckets via new `comparison_report.py` + `openpyxl` (MIT, pinned), never stored), comparison-safe CheckEdit (name+thresholds only), catalog entry + regenerated GX-contract fixture, feature-matrix row; Playwright comparison spec green against the live local stack (which also re-validated #797 — the stale api container 429'd the suite until recreated). **#794 comparison run path shipped ([#809](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/809))** — `services/comparison_run.py` builds a per-run executor bound to the suite's resolved target (source side = the check's ref + `config.source` through `resolve_target`/batch materialization or a query spec; target side = the run's materialized table or `config.target_query`), reads both via the #792 reader, diffs with the #793 engine, and maps buckets → `CheckOutcome` (mismatch-% → `metric_value` under ADR 0016 banding; counts → `observed_value`; capped samples → `sample_failures`). `_run_outcomes` routes comparison checks to the injected executor (None → per-check `error`, callers stay safe); worker builds it only when the suite has comparison checks. Failure semantics #122: reader/engine refusals + unexpected exceptions → operational `error` results (classified messages, never raw text). The sample redactor gains a comparison branch — suffix-stripped policy matching so `pii_columns: [email]` masks `email_src`+`email_tgt`, unknown columns keep default-mask. **#793 comparison engine shipped ([#808](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/808))** — `datasources/comparison.py`, the FDC record-grain diff port (ADR 0014 §2: engine yes, app no): key-joined outer-merge buckets (matched / mismatched / additional-in-source / additional-in-target) with per-column mismatch counts, typed `DuplicateKeyError` (FDC silently drop_duplicates-ed — pairing must never be ambiguous) + `ComparisonInputError`, NA-safe nullable-`string` comparison (null==null matches; a literal "nan" ≠ NULL — fixes the FDC astype(str) quirk), pairwise numeric canonicalization (numpy NULL-int→float64 "10.0" vs Arrow "10" can't fabricate mismatches; dup-key detection on normalized keys, samples echo original values), mismatch-% badness scalar over the logical-row union, capped JSON-clean sample buckets. **#792 DatasetReader seam shipped ([#807](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/807))** — `services/dataset_reader.py`: per-datasource capped DataFrame reads behind profiler-style dict dispatch (SQL via the profiler engine plumbing, flat-file object read, `pyiceberg` scan; orchestration types 422), COUNT/scan-metadata preflight + post-read race guard + typed `DatasetTooLargeError` (fail-fast, never truncation), read-time read-only re-validation of query specs, `COMPARISON_MAX_ROWS` setting (default 100k, per-check `config.max_rows` override) documented in deploy/README. **#791 schema + authoring slice shipped ([#798](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/798))** — `checks.source_connection_id` FK + kind⇔presence CHECK (migration `b7c8d9e0f1a2`, up+down tested), author-time validation (source spec via `resolve_target`, read-only per-side SQL per ADR 0019, join keys, orchestration sources rejected), RESTRICT delete guard (409 `connection_in_use`), export/import `(name, env)` round-trip, version snapshots, and the run-path guard (a comparison check yields a per-check `error` result until the #794 runner — never fails its siblings' run). User follow-up batch filed to `v1.1 Backlog` (2026-07-12): [#799](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/799) comparison:columns + optimization pass · [#800](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/800) Astronomer verification · [#801](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/801) mobile overlay nav · [#802](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/802) hierarchical asset browse · [#803](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/803) asset health split · [#804](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/804) Marquez in harness · [#805](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/805) lineage graph view · [#806](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/806) stale-nosec chore. Same session: **#796 CI-E2E 429 regression fixed via [#797](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/797)** — the #783 limiter throttled the tokenless dev-bypass Playwright suite (whole suite = one per-IP `unauth` bucket); diagnosed from the report artifact ("Too many requests" rendered in the failing pages); `RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED=false` in the CI E2E job + compose dev default. · **#725 request rate limiting shipped (PR #783)** — app-level *innermost* HTTP middleware on every public surface (REST + orchestration webhooks + the `/mcp` mount a route dependency can't reach); fixed-window Redis counter (window index in the key → no EXPIRE race), **no new dep** (`redis` INCR over slowapi/limits), **fail-open**; endpoint classes `webhook`(per-IP) · `default`(per-`sha256(token)`) · `unauth`(per-IP), raw token never keyed/logged; `/healthz`+`OPTIONS` exempt; 429 carries `Retry-After` + `detail.retry_after_seconds`; ADR [0035](adr/0035-request-rate-limiting.md); the named hard prereq for the ADR-0032 OTP sign-in (#738). · **#773 nav inversion shipped (this PR)** — G-d phase 4: the dashboard opens with an asset-health lead strip (monitored / need-attention / in-progress, severity-sorted preview, explicit first-200 truncation note), Assets moves to the top of the sidebar, suite/run detail link back to their asset, and admins get an owner-reassignment picker on AssetDetail (mount-gated so the /me-resolution race can't strand it; `/admin/users` never fetched for non-admins — structural + test-pinned). Frontend-only — the owner PATCH backend had already shipped in #772. blast radius correct both directions — closes the feature-matrix's "adapter-aware, not live-tested" caveat) and the **AssetDetail CI flake fixed** (#778 via [#780](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/780), 15s budgets). · **#768 check-editor type guidance closed** — the `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type` UX finding from the #767 live schema-drift session: added the expectation to the check-editor catalog with a datasource-tailored `type_` hint (Snowflake: exact SQL dialect type; UC/flat-file/Iceberg: Python value type name — verified per-runner execution engine, not guessed) + a [datasources-checks.md](datasources-checks.md) cheat-sheet; the dry-run preview already surfaced `observed_value` for calibration, so no new plumbing there. **G-d build COMPLETE — #762 LineageProvider/Marquez shipped** (the seam + pull + compose profile; all six G-d issues #596/#757–#762 now closed) · **#761 incidents shipped** (G-d phase 3 — lifecycle+dedup engine, evidence card, publisher payload, grant-derived incident API + AssetDetail panel; #773 nav-inversion filed as phase 4) · **W3 in progress 2026-07-11** — **#767 P1 result-misattribution fixed** ([PR #771](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/771) — GX error-first reorder vs positional zip; MCP tool explicitly deferred). Re-minted the SAS (1y), re-ran the build, and the chain fired end to end (poll → `refresh_dbt_lineage` → `lineage_edges` → graph; the trigger-binding also ran the bound suite 4/4 green). **Two systemic defects fall out, still open on [#828](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/828):** a failing poll has **no user-visible signal** and the UI's empty-lineage state is indistinguishable from "this asset has no upstreams"; and `_POLL_LOOKBACK` is 15 min, so once auth was fixed the poll succeeded and still recorded **nothing** — every build produced during the outage had aged out and is stranded in the artifact store, recoverable only by re-running the producer. [#823](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/823) narrowed to the still-unproven Marquez pull path. Shipped alongside: [#829](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/829) — a suite was **unshareable from a phone** (the Add button painted at x=407 on a 390px viewport) — and [#830](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/830) — the assets UI printed the raw OL namespace (an Iceberg DSN) instead of a human label. **Both agentic reviews earned their keep:** on #829 the review caught that the new e2e spec was **worthless** (`toBeInViewport()` defaults to `ratio: 0`, and 71% of the broken button was on screen — it passed on the bug); on #830 it caught a **missed fifth render site** (the dashboard) and that the label still **leaked the DB username** when a catalog URI had no path, which made the commit message's own claim false.) (**user follow-up batch #801–#805 all closed 2026-07-12** — the five UI/ops follow-ups filed off the ADR-0015 session: mobile overlay nav ([#817](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/817)), hierarchical asset browse ([#818](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/818)), asset-health split into connection-vs-data-quality axes ([#819](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/819)), the left-to-right lineage graph ([#820](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/820)), and the Marquez local verification + runbook correction ([#821](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/821)). Four latent bugs surfaced and were fixed en route: a run that evaluated nothing read as a green "Passing"; a `cancelled` run rolled up green on **both** health axes; an asset→asset navigation showed the *previous* asset's data (nothing could reach it before the graph's clickable nodes); and the demo seed had **no lineage at all**, so a fresh install could not show the feature. **#813 closed via [#814](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/814)** — weekly `/security-scan` OWASP spot-check found the MCP `create_check`/`update_check` tools had no Pydantic layer, so `name`/`expectation_type` could exceed the REST 256/128 bounds and hit a raw Postgres `StringDataRightTruncation` instead of a clean 422; pushed `check_service.validate_lengths` (now public) into both, extended to `suite_io_service.import_suite`'s direct `Check(...)` construction too (agentic review caught that same-class gap pre-merge). Same session: 13 open CodeQL code-scanning alerts triaged — 10 dismissed as false-positive/by-design (module-level cache globals, a documented lazy-import cycle, an intentional test canary), 3 genuine (`side-effect-in-assert` ×2, `unnecessary-lambda`) fixed in [#815](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/815). **#799 comparison:columns + tolerance + optimization shipped (this PR)** — FDC's column grain as `comparison:columns` (per-column matched/mismatched/additional-per-side VALUE buckets, dropna parity, same sample shape → redaction/report reuse), numeric `config.tolerance` {absolute, relative} on both grains (the #808-review float32 finding — one-sided NULLs never tolerance-equal), and the shared keys+positions alignment pass (×1.52 on the 1M-row benchmark, bucket parity asserted; integer keys now render "1" not "1.0" in samples); catalog fixture regenerated, per-column breakdown + tolerance fields in the UI. · **ADR [0015](adr/0015-two-connection-comparison-check-model.md) written + merged ([#790](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/790), 2026-07-12)** — the two-connection comparison check model: the suite stays single-connection and supplies the **target under test**; a `comparison` check adds one **source** ref. Closes the design gate reserved since ADR 0014; build set [#791](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/791)–[#795](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/795) filed on W3. **#795 comparison UI + report download shipped (this PR) — the ADR 0015 build is COMPLETE (#791–#795, five PRs in one session 2026-07-12)**: side-by-side check editor (source pane = connection picker + table-or-SQL per type + keys; target pane locked to the suite's connection — the §1 invariant made visible; common options below), RunDetail bucket counts + redacted sample tables + on-demand CSV/XLSX report download (derived server-side from the redacted buckets via new `comparison_report.py` + `openpyxl` (MIT, pinned), never stored), comparison-safe CheckEdit (name+thresholds only), catalog entry + regenerated GX-contract fixture, feature-matrix row; Playwright comparison spec green against the live local stack (which also re-validated #797 — the stale api container 429'd the suite until recreated). **#794 comparison run path shipped ([#809](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/809))** — `services/comparison_run.py` builds a per-run executor bound to the suite's resolved target (source side = the check's ref + `config.source` through `resolve_target`/batch materialization or a query spec; target side = the run's materialized table or `config.target_query`), reads both via the #792 reader, diffs with the #793 engine, and maps buckets → `CheckOutcome` (mismatch-% → `metric_value` under ADR 0016 banding; counts → `observed_value`; capped samples → `sample_failures`). `_run_outcomes` routes comparison checks to the injected executor (None → per-check `error`, callers stay safe); worker builds it only when the suite has comparison checks. Failure semantics #122: reader/engine refusals + unexpected exceptions → operational `error` results (classified messages, never raw text). The sample redactor gains a comparison branch — suffix-stripped policy matching so `pii_columns: [email]` masks `email_src`+`email_tgt`, unknown columns keep default-mask. **#793 comparison engine shipped ([#808](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/808))** — `datasources/comparison.py`, the FDC record-grain diff port (ADR 0014 §2: engine yes, app no): key-joined outer-merge buckets (matched / mismatched / additional-in-source / additional-in-target) with per-column mismatch counts, typed `DuplicateKeyError` (FDC silently drop_duplicates-ed — pairing must never be ambiguous) + `ComparisonInputError`, NA-safe nullable-`string` comparison (null==null matches; a literal "nan" ≠ NULL — fixes the FDC astype(str) quirk), pairwise numeric canonicalization (numpy NULL-int→float64 "10.0" vs Arrow "10" can't fabricate mismatches; dup-key detection on normalized keys, samples echo original values), mismatch-% badness scalar over the logical-row union, capped JSON-clean sample buckets. **#792 DatasetReader seam shipped ([#807](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/807))** — `services/dataset_reader.py`: per-datasource capped DataFrame reads behind profiler-style dict dispatch (SQL via the profiler engine plumbing, flat-file object read, `pyiceberg` scan; orchestration types 422), COUNT/scan-metadata preflight + post-read race guard + typed `DatasetTooLargeError` (fail-fast, never truncation), read-time read-only re-validation of query specs, `COMPARISON_MAX_ROWS` setting (default 100k, per-check `config.max_rows` override) documented in deploy/README. **#791 schema + authoring slice shipped ([#798](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/798))** — `checks.source_connection_id` FK + kind⇔presence CHECK (migration `b7c8d9e0f1a2`, up+down tested), author-time validation (source spec via `resolve_target`, read-only per-side SQL per ADR 0019, join keys, orchestration sources rejected), RESTRICT delete guard (409 `connection_in_use`), export/import `(name, env)` round-trip, version snapshots, and the run-path guard (a comparison check yields a per-check `error` result until the #794 runner — never fails its siblings' run). content.text: Updating the ledger on the #428 branch (rides its PR), then committing. First the two Snapshot edits:

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
src = open('docs/progress.md').read()
old_open_issues = "| **Open issues** | **33** in the **`v1.1 Backlog`** milestone (76 open repo-wide)"
new_open_issues = "| **Open issues** | **30** in the **`v1.1 Backlog`** milestone (77 open repo-wide)"
assert old_open_issues in src
src = src.replace(old_open_issues, new_open_issues, 1)
marker = "(**2026-07-13 — prod lineage restored"
note = "(**2026-07-14 — #785 per-provider webhook rate-limit buckets shipped ([#872](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/872)):** the webhook class is now dual-key — a per provider+IP bucket (adf/airflow/dbt folded into the key, unknown segments share one bare-IP bucket so a scanner can't mint buckets) **plus** a `rl:webhook:ipall:{ip}` aggregate ceiling (`RATE_LIMIT_WEBHOOK_IP_PER_MINUTE`, default 240) the agentic review added — without it, per-provider folding would have quietly multiplied the per-IP webhook budget by 4. The review also pinned the fail-open log's `key_kind` to the documented `{tok, ip}` domain (the post-#852 observability-drift lesson) and replaced a hand-copied provider frozenset with the public `db.models.ORCHESTRATION_PROVIDERS` — the claimed layering obstacle was false. ADR 0035 + deploy/README synced, incl. the new Redis SCAN pattern `rl:webhook:*:ip:*`.) "
assert marker in src
src = src.replace(marker, note + marker, 1)
old_prs = "| **Open PRs** | none |"
assert old_prs in src
src = src.replace(old_prs, "| **Open PRs** | 12 — the 2026-07-13 Dependabot version-bump batch (#860–#871), pending the usual deliberate batch review |", 1)
open('docs/progress.md','w').write(src)
print("ok")
EOF

input.command:
git add backend/app/datasources/sql.py backend/app/datasources/monitors.py backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py backend/app/datasources/unity_catalog.py backend/app/services/profile_service.py backend/tests/datasources/test_sql.py && git commit -m "refactor(datasources): one SQL-identifier allowlist + one run_monitors engine loop (#428)

Two byte-identical copies of the plain-identifier regex (monitors._IDENT_RE,
profile_service._IDENTIFIER) and two near-line-identical run_monitors
engine/connection/scalar bodies (Snowflake, Unity Catalog) collapse into a
new backend/app/datasources/sql.py:

- SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE / is_sql_identifier — the single allowlist decision;
  callers keep their own error types (MonitorConfigError vs the profiler's
  422). Widening the set now happens everywhere at once.
- run_monitors_over_engine(engine, ...) — the shared open-one-connection →
  evaluate_monitors loop; the runners now differ only in URL/engine
  construction (incl. the SF-only schema fallback, which stays at its call
  site). The caller owns the engine lifecycle — the seam #427 will thread a
  per-run shared engine through.

Behavior unchanged; monitors.py stays connection-free (the engine helper
lazy-imports it, avoiding a cycle).

Fixes #428

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>" && git push -u origin refactor/issue-428-shared-sql-ident && gh pr create --title "refactor(datasources): one SQL-identifier allowlist + one run_monitors engine loop (#428)" --milestone "v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind" --body "## Summary

Fixes #428 (surfaced by the #426 code review; W4 prep — this is the code #592 \`schema_drift\` builds on).

## Changes

- **New \`backend/app/datasources/sql.py\`** — shared SQL-datasource primitives:
  - \`SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE\` / \`is_sql_identifier\`: the one plain-identifier allowlist (Snowflake/Databricks unquoted set). \`monitors._ident\` and \`profile_service.validate_identifier\` both route through it; each keeps its own error type (\`MonitorConfigError\` vs 422 \`ProfileIdentifierInvalidError\`). The regex copies are deleted.
  - \`run_monitors_over_engine(engine, ...)\`: the engine → one connection → \`evaluate_monitors\` scalar loop the SF/UC runners previously duplicated. Runners now differ only in URL/engine construction; the SF-only \`schema or self._config.schema_\` fallback stays at its call site. Caller owns the engine lifecycle — the seam #427 threads a per-run shared engine through next.
- \`monitors.py\` remains connection-free (its documented property); the helper lazy-imports \`evaluate_monitors\` to avoid the cycle.
- Note: the issue's third copy (custom-SQL path) no longer exists — it was refactored away since #426; only the two regex copies remained.

## Acceptance criteria (from #428)

- [x] SQL-identifier validation consolidated to one helper; \`run_monitors\` boilerplate deduped across SQL runners
- [x] Existing monitor tests green

## Testing

- New \`backend/tests/datasources/test_sql.py\`: allowlist accept/reject (incl. non-strings), a pin that BOTH consumers route through the shared decision, and \`run_monitors_over_engine\` over a real sqlite engine (happy path, per-monitor error isolation, connection-failure propagation).
- Monitors / snowflake / unity_catalog / profile_service suites green (207 tests); full backend suite green locally.

Also carries the docs/progress.md ledger flip for the just-merged #872 (#785) + Snapshot recount (next commit).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)"

content.text: While the #873 reviewers run, starting the #726 + #429 design — reading the dispatch surfaces:

content.content:
1	"""Datasource adapter seam.
2	
3	Every datasource (Snowflake now; ADLS / S3 / Unity Catalog later) executes DQ
4	checks behind one ``CheckRunner`` interface that speaks GX-agnostic DTOs. The
5	GX-specific machinery lives entirely inside each adapter, so the run-service and
6	its tests depend only on the types here — never on Great Expectations internals.
7	This is also the seam that lets v1.1 swap GX for DQX on Unity Catalog (CLAUDE.md
8	§5) without rippling into the suite / check / result layer.
9	
10	`CheckSpec` goes in (a check pulled from the DB); `CheckOutcome` comes out, one
11	per check, shaped to map cleanly onto the `results` table columns. Adapters
12	translate GX results into these DTOs; tests provide a fake `CheckRunner` and
13	never touch a live datasource.
14	"""
15	
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	from dataclasses import dataclass
19	from typing import Any, Protocol, runtime_checkable
20	
21	from pydantic import BaseModel
22	
23	
24	@dataclass(frozen=True)
25	class CheckSpec:
26	    """One expectation to evaluate, sourced from a `checks` row.
27	
28	    `expectation_type` is the GX snake_case name (e.g.
29	    ``expect_column_values_to_not_be_null``); `kwargs` are its parameters
30	    (e.g. ``{"column": "id"}``). Adapters own the translation to the concrete
31	    GX expectation class.
32	    """
33	
34	    expectation_type: str
35	    kwargs: dict[str, Any]
36	
37	
38	@dataclass(frozen=True)
39	class CheckOutcome:
40	    """Result of one check, shaped for the `results` table.
41	
42	    `observed_value` / `expected_value` / `sample_failures` land in the
43	    matching JSONB columns. `sample_failures` may contain real data rows, so it
44	    is governed by the retention sweep and must only ever be logged through the
45	    PII-redacting structlog chain.
46	
47	    `errored` marks a check that could not be *evaluated* (the runner caught an
48	    exception while computing it — e.g. it references a missing column), as
49	    opposed to a check that evaluated and *failed* (`success=False`). The two are
50	    distinct result statuses (#122): an errored check maps to ``error`` (no
51	    severity, no metric), a failed one to a severity tier. A single errored check
52	    never fails its siblings — they still evaluate and persist.
53	    """
54	
55	    expectation_type: str
56	    success: bool
57	    observed_value: dict[str, Any] | None = None
58	    expected_value: dict[str, Any] | None = None
59	    sample_failures: dict[str, Any] | None = None
60	    errored: bool = False
61	    error_message: str | None = None
62	    # The badness scalar a *monitor* (freshness/volume, ADR 0012) computed directly
63	    # — age-hours, % volume deviation. `severity.extract_metric` prefers this when
64	    # set, so monitor kinds band the same way (higher = worse, ADR 0016) without
65	    # abusing the GX unexpected-% sample shape. None for GX expectations, whose
66	    # metric is parsed from the sample.
67	    metric_value: float | None = None
68	
69	
70	@dataclass(frozen=True)
71	class MonitorSpec:
72	    """One monitor to evaluate (freshness/volume, ADR 0012), sourced from a `checks`
73	    row whose ``kind`` is a monitor kind. ``config`` is the check's JSONB config
74	    (e.g. ``{"column": "loaded_at"}`` / ``{"min_rows": 1000, "max_rows": 5000}``).
75	
76	    A monitor isn't a GX expectation — it runs a scalar SQL aggregate — so it has its
77	    own spec/runner path distinct from `CheckSpec`/`CheckRunner`.
78	    """
79	
80	    kind: str
81	    config: dict[str, Any]
82	
83	
84	@dataclass(frozen=True)
85	class SuiteOutcome:
86	    """Aggregate result of running a list of checks against one table."""
87	
88	    success: bool
89	    checks: list[CheckOutcome]
90	
91	
92	@runtime_checkable
93	class CheckRunner(Protocol):
94	    """Executes a set of checks against a single table and returns outcomes."""
95	
96	    def run_checks(
97	        self,
98	        *,
99	        table: str,
100	        schema: str | None,
101	        checks: list[CheckSpec],
102	        index_columns: list[str] | None = None,
103	    ) -> SuiteOutcome: ...
104	
105	
106	@runtime_checkable
107	class MonitorRunner(Protocol):
108	    """A datasource runner that can also evaluate **monitor** kinds (freshness/
109	    volume) by running scalar SQL aggregates against the table — the SQL datasources
110	    (Snowflake, Unity Catalog) in v1. Flat-file runners don't implement this, so the
111	    run path can gate monitor checks to SQL datasources via an ``isinstance`` check.
112	
113	    One ``CheckOutcome`` per ``MonitorSpec``, in order. A monitor that can't be
114	    evaluated (bad column, type mismatch) yields an ``errored`` outcome rather than
115	    failing its siblings — mirroring `CheckRunner` semantics.
116	    """
117	
118	    def run_monitors(
119	        self,
120	        *,
121	        table: str,
122	        schema: str | None,
123	        monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
124	    ) -> list[CheckOutcome]: ...
125	
126	
127	@runtime_checkable
128	class ConnectionAdapter(Protocol):
129	    """Per-datasource-type connection behaviour: config validation + live test.
130	
131	    The two things that vary across connection types (Snowflake now; ADF, ADLS,
132	    S3, Unity Catalog next) behind one interface, so connection-CRUD service code
133	    dispatches by ``connection.type`` and never branches on it. Each adapter owns
134	    its own pydantic config model; both methods take the raw config dict so the
135	    adapter is the single source of truth for that type's shape.
136	
137	    `validate_config` parses + validates a stored/incoming config (raising
138	    pydantic ``ValidationError`` on bad input) and returns the normalised model.
139	    `test` resolves connectivity against the live datasource using the config +
140	    its secret, raising on failure. Adapters never touch the DB or SecretStore —
141	    the caller resolves the secret and hands it in.
142	
143	    Some types need MORE than one credential (an Iceberg SQL catalog needs the
144	    storage key *and* the catalog DB password). Rather than smuggle the second one
145	    into non-secret `config` — the #754/#826 bug — the caller resolves every extra
146	    secret named in config and passes them as keyword arguments. Adapters that need
147	    none simply ignore them (`**_`), so the seam's "caller resolves secrets"
148	    invariant holds for one credential or five.
149	    """
150

file.content:
"""Datasource adapter seam.

Every datasource (Snowflake now; ADLS / S3 / Unity Catalog later) executes DQ
checks behind one ``CheckRunner`` interface that speaks GX-agnostic DTOs. The
GX-specific machinery lives entirely inside each adapter, so the run-service and
its tests depend only on the types here — never on Great Expectations internals.
This is also the seam that lets v1.1 swap GX for DQX on Unity Catalog (CLAUDE.md
§5) without rippling into the suite / check / result layer.

`CheckSpec` goes in (a check pulled from the DB); `CheckOutcome` comes out, one
per check, shaped to map cleanly onto the `results` table columns. Adapters
translate GX results into these DTOs; tests provide a fake `CheckRunner` and
never touch a live datasource.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any, Protocol, runtime_checkable

from pydantic import BaseModel


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class CheckSpec:
    """One expectation to evaluate, sourced from a `checks` row.

    `expectation_type` is the GX snake_case name (e.g.
    ``expect_column_values_to_not_be_null``); `kwargs` are its parameters
    (e.g. ``{"column": "id"}``). Adapters own the translation to the concrete
    GX expectation class.
    """

    expectation_type: str
    kwargs: dict[str, Any]


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class CheckOutcome:
    """Result of one check, shaped for the `results` table.

    `observed_value` / `expected_value` / `sample_failures` land in the
    matching JSONB columns. `sample_failures` may contain real data rows, so it
    is governed by the retention sweep and must only ever be logged through the
    PII-redacting structlog chain.

    `errored` marks a check that could not be *evaluated* (the runner caught an
    exception while computing it — e.g. it references a missing column), as
    opposed to a check that evaluated and *failed* (`success=False`). The two are
    distinct result statuses (#122): an errored check maps to ``error`` (no
    severity, no metric), a failed one to a severity tier. A single errored check
    never fails its siblings — they still evaluate and persist.
    """

    expectation_type: str
    success: bool
    observed_value: dict[str, Any] | None = None
    expected_value: dict[str, Any] | None = None
    sample_failures: dict[str, Any] | None = None
    errored: bool = False
    error_message: str | None = None
    # The badness scalar a *monitor* (freshness/volume, ADR 0012) computed directly
    # — age-hours, % volume deviation. `severity.extract_metric` prefers this when
    # set, so monitor kinds band the same way (higher = worse, ADR 0016) without
    # abusing the GX unexpected-% sample shape. None for GX expectations, whose
    # metric is parsed from the sample.
    metric_value: float | None = None


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class MonitorSpec:
    """One monitor to evaluate (freshness/volume, ADR 0012), sourced from a `checks`
    row whose ``kind`` is a monitor kind. ``config`` is the check's JSONB config
    (e.g. ``{"column": "loaded_at"}`` / ``{"min_rows": 1000, "max_rows": 5000}``).

    A monitor isn't a GX expectation — it runs a scalar SQL aggregate — so it has its
    own spec/runner path distinct from `CheckSpec`/`CheckRunner`.
    """

    kind: str
    config: dict[str, Any]


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class SuiteOutcome:
    """Aggregate result of running a list of checks against one table."""

    success: bool
    checks: list[CheckOutcome]


@runtime_checkable
class CheckRunner(Protocol):
    """Executes a set of checks against a single table and returns outcomes."""

    def run_checks(
        self,
        *,
        table: str,
        schema: str | None,
        checks: list[CheckSpec],
        index_columns: list[str] | None = None,
    ) -> SuiteOutcome: ...


@runtime_checkable
class MonitorRunner(Protocol):
    """A datasource runner that can also evaluate **monitor** kinds (freshness/
    volume) by running scalar SQL aggregates against the table — the SQL datasources
    (Snowflake, Unity Catalog) in v1. Flat-file runners don't implement this, so the
    run path can gate monitor checks to SQL datasources via an ``isinstance`` check.

    One ``CheckOutcome`` per ``MonitorSpec``, in order. A monitor that can't be
    evaluated (bad column, type mismatch) yields an ``errored`` outcome rather than
    failing its siblings — mirroring `CheckRunner` semantics.
    """

    def run_monitors(
        self,
        *,
        table: str,
        schema: str | None,
        monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
    ) -> list[CheckOutcome]: ...


@runtime_checkable
class ConnectionAdapter(Protocol):
    """Per-datasource-type connection behaviour: config validation + live test.

    The two things that vary across connection types (Snowflake now; ADF, ADLS,
    S3, Unity Catalog next) behind one interface, so connection-CRUD service code
    dispatches by ``connection.type`` and never branches on it. Each adapter owns
    its own pydantic config model; both methods take the raw config dict so the
    adapter is the single source of truth for that type's shape.

    `validate_config` parses + validates a stored/incoming config (raising
    pydantic ``ValidationError`` on bad input) and returns the normalised model.
    `test` resolves connectivity against the live datasource using the config +
    its secret, raising on failure. Adapters never touch the DB or SecretStore —
    the caller resolves the secret and hands it in.

    Some types need MORE than one credential (an Iceberg SQL catalog needs the
    storage key *and* the catalog DB password). Rather than smuggle the second one
    into non-secret `config` — the #754/#826 bug — the caller resolves every extra
    secret named in config and passes them as keyword arguments. Adapters that need
    none simply ignore them (`**_`), so the seam's "caller resolves secrets"
    invariant holds for one credential or five.
    """

    def validate_config(self, raw: dict[str, Any]) -> BaseModel: ...

    def test(self, raw: dict[str, Any], secret: str, **extra_secrets: Any) -> None: ...

content.content:
Found by the 2026-07-09 architecture review (findings register issue links back here). Filed into W4 deliberately: this refactor should land **before or with** schema_drift (#592) so the new kind is an additive registration, not a fourth copy of the if-chains.

## Problem

The monitor-kind seam (ADR 0012) is extensible in the *schema* but not in the *code*. Adding a kind today touches at least three sites:

1. `run_service._run_outcomes` (`backend/app/services/run_service.py:101-156`) — hardcoded two-way partition (`expectation` vs `MONITOR_KINDS`), `NotImplementedError` for the rest.
2. `check_service._V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS` (`backend/app/services/check_service.py:55`) — authoring-side allowlist.
3. `backend/app/datasources/monitors.py` — **three parallel `if kind == FRESHNESS / VOLUME / else raise` chains** repeated across `build_monitor_sql`, `validate_monitor_config`, and `monitor_outcome` (~lines 104-109, 171-176, 192-231).

Fine at 2 kinds; at 4 (schema_drift #592, anomaly #593) the parallel chains become a drift/bug surface — a kind added to one function but not another fails at runtime, not import time.

## Proposed direction

A per-kind strategy object (e.g. `MonitorKindSpec`: `validate_config`, `build_sql` / scalar-source hook, `outcome`) in a registry keyed by kind — mirroring how `_ADAPTERS`/`_RUNNER_BUILDERS` already work in `datasources/registry.py`. `monitors.py`'s three chains collapse into lookups; `_V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS` derives from the registry. Keep the `expectation`-vs-monitor split in `_run_outcomes` (it dispatches to a different runner Protocol) but make the monitor side registry-driven.

Note the Iceberg runner variation: it feeds `run_monitor_specs` from native scan scalars instead of SQL aggregates — the strategy interface must keep that scalar-source seam open (it is today's one intended point of variation).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Adding a monitor kind = one registry entry + one strategy class (no edits to `monitors.py` chains or `_V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS`)
- [ ] Reserved-kind rejection behavior unchanged (graceful 4xx at authoring, no runtime surprises)
- [ ] Existing freshness/volume behavior covered by the current test suite, green unchanged
- [ ] #592 (schema_drift) implemented on top of the new seam

1	"""Monitor kinds — freshness & volume (ADR 0012), the non-GX computed monitors.
2	
3	A monitor isn't a GX expectation: it runs a single **scalar SQL aggregate** against
4	the target table and turns the result into a badness ``metric_value`` that the
5	severity layer bands (higher = worse, ADR 0016), exactly like a GX check's
6	unexpected-%. This module is the pure, datasource-agnostic core:
7	
8	* :func:`build_monitor_sql` — the aggregate query a SQL runner executes;
9	* :func:`monitor_outcome` — scalar result + check config → ``CheckOutcome``.
10	
11	The per-datasource *execution* (open a connection, run the SQL, fetch the scalar)
12	lives in the SQL runners; this module never touches a connection, so it is fully
13	unit-tested. v1 monitors are SQL-datasource only (Snowflake / Unity Catalog).
14	
15	Semantics (locked):
16	* **freshness** — config ``{"column": <timestamp col>}``; metric = **age in hours**
17	  of ``MAX(column)`` vs now (higher = staler = worse). Banded by the check's
18	  warn/fail/critical thresholds (e.g. warn 24h, fail 48h).
19	* **volume** — config ``{"min_rows": N, "max_rows": M}``; metric = **% deviation**
20	  of ``COUNT(*)`` *outside* ``[N, M]`` (either direction; 0 when in range). Banded
21	  by the thresholds, so a drop *or* a spike past tolerance escalates.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import re
27	from collections.abc import Callable
28	from datetime import UTC, date, datetime, time
29	from typing import Any
30	
31	from backend.app.datasources.base import CheckOutcome, MonitorSpec
32	
33	FRESHNESS = "freshness"
34	VOLUME = "volume"
35	MONITOR_KINDS = (FRESHNESS, VOLUME)
36	
37	# A monitor's `expectation_type` slot records the kind (the column is GX-shaped but
38	# monitors aren't GX); `monitor:<kind>` keeps it self-describing on the result row.
39	_EXPECTATION_PREFIX = "monitor:"
40	
41	
42	def monitor_expectation_type(kind: str) -> str:
43	    """The canonical ``expectation_type`` for a monitor kind — ``monitor:<kind>``.
44	
45	    The single source of truth shared by the run path (stamps it on result rows),
46	    the author path (asserts the stored check's type matches its kind), and the
47	    frontend catalog — so the kind↔type pairing can't drift."""
48	    return f"{_EXPECTATION_PREFIX}{kind}"
49	
50	
51	# SQL identifier we're willing to interpolate into the aggregate. Monitor config is
52	# user-authored, so the column/table/schema must be validated before they touch a
53	# query string (no bound-param slot for an identifier). Snowflake/Databricks
54	# unquoted identifiers: a letter/underscore lead, then word chars or `$`.
55	_IDENT_RE = re.compile(r"^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_$]*$")
56	
57	
58	class MonitorConfigError(ValueError):
59	    """A monitor check's config is missing/invalid (bad column, range, or kind)."""
60	
61	
62	def _ident(name: object, *, what: str) -> str:
63	    """Validate a SQL identifier (so it's safe to interpolate) and return it."""
64	    if not isinstance(name, str) or not _IDENT_RE.match(name):
65	        raise MonitorConfigError(f"invalid {what} identifier: {name!r}")
66	    return name
67	
68	
69	def qualified_table(*, table: str, schema: str | None, catalog: str | None) -> str:
70	    """A dotted, identifier-validated ``[catalog.][schema.]table`` for a monitor query.
71	
72	    A ``catalog`` with no ``schema`` is rejected: skipping the None ``schema`` would
73	    emit a 2-part ``catalog.table``, which Databricks/Unity Catalog resolves as
74	    ``schema.table`` (wrong object), not the intended 3-part name. So a catalog
75	    requires a schema — a misqualified-name footgun raised as a clear config error
76	    rather than a confusing "table not found" at query time."""
77	    if catalog is not None and schema is None:
78	        raise MonitorConfigError(
79	            f"monitor target {table!r} has a catalog but no schema — "
80	            "a catalog needs a schema (else catalog.table misresolves as schema.table)"
81	        )
82	    parts = [
83	        _ident(part, what=label)
84	        for part, label in ((catalog, "catalog"), (schema, "schema"), (table, "table"))
85	        if part is not None
86	    ]
87	    return ".".join(parts)
88	
89	
90	def build_monitor_sql(
91	    kind: str, *, table: str, schema: str | None, catalog: str | None, config: dict[str, Any]
92	) -> str:
93	    """The scalar-aggregate SQL a SQL runner executes for this monitor.
94	
95	    ``freshness`` → ``SELECT MAX(<column>) ...``; ``volume`` → ``SELECT COUNT(*) ...``.
96	    Identifiers are validated (no bind slot for them), so a bad column/table raises
97	    :class:`MonitorConfigError` rather than building an injectable query.
98	    """
99	    target = qualified_table(table=table, schema=schema, catalog=catalog)
100	    # `column` + every part of `target` are validated by `_ident` (strict identifier
101	    # regex) before interpolation. SQL identifiers can't be bound parameters, so
102	    # validated interpolation is the correct construction — not an injection vector
103	    # (hence the S608 suppressions).
104	    if kind == FRESHNESS:
105	        column = _ident(config.get("column"), what="freshness column")
106	        return f"SELECT MAX({column}) FROM {target}"  # noqa: S608  # nosec B608
107	    if kind == VOLUME:
108	        return f"SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {target}"  # noqa: S608  # nosec B608
109	    raise MonitorConfigError(f"unknown monitor kind: {kind!r}")
110	
111	
112	def _freshness_age_hours(max_timestamp: datetime, now: datetime) -> float:
113	    """Hours between ``MAX(column)`` and now (clamped at 0 — a clock-skew future
114	    timestamp isn't 'negatively stale')."""
115	    return max((now - max_timestamp).total_seconds() / 3600.0, 0.0)
116	
117	
118	def _as_aware_datetime(scalar: object, column: str) -> datetime:
119	    """Normalise a ``MAX(column)`` scalar to a tz-aware datetime for the age math.
120	
121	    Accepts a ``datetime`` *or* a ``date`` (a DATE column's MAX is a ``date`` — e.g.
122	    Snowflake ``SIGNUP_DATE`` → ``datetime.date``; midnight is used). A naive value
123	    (Snowflake ``TIMESTAMP_NTZ`` returns no tzinfo) is assumed UTC, so subtracting a
124	    UTC ``now`` never raises offset-naive-vs-aware."""
125	    if isinstance(scalar, datetime):
126	        ts = scalar
127	    elif isinstance(scalar, date):  # a plain date (datetime is a date subclass — checked first)
128	        ts = datetime.combine(scalar, time.min)
129	    else:
130	        raise MonitorConfigError(
131	            f"freshness column {column!r} is not a date/timestamp (got {type(scalar).__name__})"
132	        )
133	    return ts if ts.tzinfo is not None else ts.replace(tzinfo=UTC)
134	
135	
136	def _volume_deviation_pct(row_count: int, *, min_rows: int, max_rows: int) -> float:
137	    """Percent the row count falls **outside** ``[min_rows, max_rows]`` (0 in range).
138	
139	    Below the floor → shortfall vs the floor; above the ceiling → excess vs the
140	    ceiling. Symmetric so a drop and a spike both escalate. Guards a zero bound."""
141	    if row_count < min_rows:
142	        return (min_rows - row_count) / min_rows * 100.0 if min_rows else 100.0
143	    if row_count > max_rows:
144	        return (row_count - max_rows) / max_rows * 100.0 if max_rows else 100.0
145	    return 0.0
146	
147	
148	def _volume_bounds(config: dict[str, Any]) -> tuple[int, int]:
149	    """Validate the ``min_rows``/``max_rows`` range from a volume check's config."""
150	    try:
151	        min_rows = int(config["min_rows"])
152	        max_rows = int(config["max_rows"])
153	    except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) as exc:
154	        raise MonitorConfigError(f"volume needs integer min_rows/max_rows: {config!r}") from exc
155	    if min_rows < 0 or max_rows < min_rows:
156	        raise MonitorConfigError(f"volume range must be 0 <= min_rows <= max_rows: {config!r}")
157	    return min_rows, max_rows
158	
159	
160	def validate_monitor_config(kind: str, config: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
161	    """Static (DB-free) validation of a monitor check's ``config`` — the *structural*
162	    checks that don't need a live query: a valid ``column`` identifier (freshness) or

1	"""Snowflake datasource adapter (GX Core 1.17).
2	
3	All Great-Expectations-specific machinery for Snowflake lives here, behind the
4	`CheckRunner` seam in ``base.py`` — per CLAUDE.md, the GX version-specific API
5	must not leak into the suite / check / result layer (GX v1 has drifted across
6	point releases).
7	
8	The full GX chain (``add_snowflake`` → ``add_table_asset`` →
9	``add_batch_definition_whole_table`` → ``ValidationDefinition.run``) connects to
10	Snowflake at asset-build time, so ``run_checks`` cannot run without a live
11	warehouse. Tests therefore exercise the GX-free parts directly — config
12	validation, connection-string building, the snake_case→GX-class translation,
13	and the GX-result→`CheckOutcome` mapping (fed a canned result) — and inject a
14	fake `CheckRunner` elsewhere. End-to-end validation against a real Snowflake DEV
15	warehouse is a tracked follow-up.
16	"""

57	class SnowflakeConfig(BaseModel):
58	    """Non-secret Snowflake connection config (the password comes from secrets).
59	
60	    Maps from ``Connection.config``. ``schema`` is aliased to ``schema_`` to
61	    avoid shadowing pydantic's ``BaseModel.schema``.
62	    """
63	
64	    model_config = ConfigDict(populate_by_name=True, extra="forbid")
65	
66	    account: str
67	    user: str
68	    database: str
69	    schema_: str = Field(alias="schema")
70	    warehouse: str
71	    role: str | None = None
72	    # Auth method. 'password' (default — back-compat for existing configs that
73	    # carry no auth_type) puts the password in the DSN. 'key_pair' authenticates
74	    # with an RSA private key passed as `private_key` connect-arg, and the DSN
75	    # carries no password; the secret is either a bare PEM key or the JSON
76	    # payload for passphrase-protected keys (see `_parse_key_pair_secret`).
77	    # Both auth methods share the same SecretStore `secret_ref`.
78	    auth_type: Literal["password", "key_pair"] = "password"
79	
80	    @model_validator(mode="after")
81	    def _key_pair_requires_role(self) -> SnowflakeConfig:
82	        """Key-pair connections must carry a role.
83	
84	        GX's key-pair form (`KeyPairConnectionDetails`, #195) mandates one, so a
85	        role-less key-pair connection could never run a suite. Enforcing it here
86	        makes the failure a clear 422 at create/edit/test time instead of an
87	        opaque failed run later. Password auth keeps role optional.
88	        """
89	        if self.auth_type == "key_pair" and not self.role:
90	            raise ValueError("key-pair auth requires 'role' (suite runs mandate it)")
91	        return self
92	
93	
94	def _parse_key_pair_secret(secret: str) -> tuple[str, bytes | None]:
95	    """Split a key-pair secret payload into (PEM key, passphrase bytes or None).
96	
97	    Two shapes are accepted: a bare PEM string (an unencrypted key — the
98	    original v1 form, unchanged) or a JSON object
99	    ``{"private_key": "<PEM>", "passphrase": "<str>"}`` for passphrase-protected
100	    keys. One SecretStore entry carries both parts so the connection keeps a
101	    single `secret_ref` and rotation via re-auth stays atomic (#194).
102	
103	    Error messages never include payload content — they can carry key material.
104	    """
105	    if not secret.lstrip().startswith("{"):
106	        return secret, None
107	    try:
108	        payload = json.loads(secret)
109	    except json.JSONDecodeError as exc:
110	        raise ValueError("key-pair secret payload is not valid JSON") from exc
111	    if not isinstance(payload, dict) or not isinstance(payload.get("private_key"), str):
112	        raise ValueError("key-pair secret payload must carry a 'private_key' string")
113	    passphrase = payload.get("passphrase")
114	    if passphrase is not None and not isinstance(passphrase, str):
115	        raise ValueError("key-pair secret 'passphrase' must be a string")
116	    return payload["private_key"], passphrase.encode() if passphrase else None
117	
118	
119	def _private_key_der(secret: str) -> bytes:
120	    """Load the key-pair secret → DER PKCS8 bytes.
121	
122	    snowflake-connector's `private_key` connect-arg wants DER PKCS8, not PEM.
123	    Passphrase-protected keys arrive as the JSON payload
124	    (see `_parse_key_pair_secret`); bare PEM means an unencrypted key.
125	    """
126	    pem, passphrase = _parse_key_pair_secret(secret)
127	    try:
128	        key = serialization.load_pem_private_key(pem.encode(), password=passphrase)
129	    except TypeError as exc:
130	        # cryptography reports passphrase-presence mismatches (encrypted key
131	        # without a passphrase, or a passphrase for an unencrypted key) as
132	        # TypeError; normalise to ValueError so callers see one failure type.
133	        # Its messages state the mismatch only — no key/passphrase material.
134	        raise ValueError(str(exc)) from exc
135	    return key.private_bytes(
136	        encoding=serialization.Encoding.DER,
137	        format=serialization.PrivateFormat.PKCS8,
138	        encryption_algorithm=serialization.NoEncryption(),
139	    )

144	
145	    For key-pair auth the DSN carries **no password** (the key rides in
146	    connect-args, see `build_connect_args`); ``secret`` is then ignored here.
147	    """
148	    params = {"warehouse": config.warehouse}
149	    if config.role:
150	        params["role"] = config.role
151	    query = "&".join(f"{key}={quote_plus(value)}" for key, value in params.items())
152	    credentials = (
153	        quote_plus(config.user)
154	        if config.auth_type == "key_pair"
155	        else f"{quote_plus(config.user)}:{quote_plus(secret)}"
156	    )
157	    return f"snowflake://{credentials}@{config.account}/{config.database}/{config.schema_}?{query}"
158	
159	
160	def build_connect_args(config: SnowflakeConfig, secret: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
161	    """SQLAlchemy `connect_args` carrying the key-pair credential, if any.
162	
163	    Empty for password auth (the password is in the DSN). For key-pair, the
164	    loaded DER private key under `private_key` (the snowflake-connector arg).
165	    """
166	    if config.auth_type == "key_pair":
167	        return {"private_key": _private_key_der(secret)}
168	    return {}

# ── Rate limiting (#725, ADR 0035) ───────────────────────────────────────
    # Fixed-window (60s) request throttle on every public surface — REST, the
    # orchestration webhooks, and the mounted /mcp app — keyed per sha256(bearer)
    # for authenticated traffic and per client-IP otherwise. Fail-open (a Redis
    # outage disables limiting, logged). Defaults are generous; tighten
    # RATE_LIMIT_WEBHOOK_PER_MINUTE to your orchestrator's callback cadence.
    rate_limit_enabled: bool = True
    rate_limit_authenticated_per_minute: int = 300  # per sha256(bearer) bucket
    rate_limit_unauthenticated_per_minute: int = 120  # per client-IP bucket
    rate_limit_webhook_per_minute: int = (
        120  # per provider + client-IP bucket, /api/v1/orchestration/events/* (#785) —
        # each provider (adf/airflow/dbt) has its own bucket at this cap, NOT a
        # per-IP total; the total is rate_limit_webhook_ip_per_minute below.
    )
    rate_limit_webhook_ip_per_minute: int = (
        240  # per-IP ceiling across ALL webhook buckets from one IP (#785) — bounds
        # the aggregate a single IP can spend across provider buckets.
    )
    rate_limit_ip_per_minute: int = (
        1200  # per-IP ceiling across all bearer buckets (rotated-token backstop)
    )
    rate_limit_xff_trusted_hops: int = (
        1  # count of trusted proxies appending XFF; pick entry hops-from-right
    )

    # ── Comparison checks (ADR 0015) ─────────────────────────────────────────
    # Default row cap per comparison side. Both sides materialize in worker
    # memory for the diff (#793), so this is a memory guardrail, not a tuning
    # knob — over-cap runs fail fast (never a silently truncated diff). A check
    # may override via config.max_rows; scale-aware execution (G-b) is the path

file.content:
def _strip_ipv4_port(host: str) -> str:
    match = _IPV4_PORT_RE.match(host)
    return match.group(1) if match else host


def _is_ip(value: str) -> bool:
    try:
        ipaddress.ip_address(value)
        return True
    except ValueError:
        return False


def _client_ip(request: Request, trusted_hops: int) -> str:
    """The client IP for per-IP buckets.

    X-Forwarded-For is a comma-separated chain — each trusted proxy appends the
    peer it saw. Exactly `trusted_hops` proxies are trusted to append (config
    `RATE_LIMIT_XFF_TRUSTED_HOPS`), so the real client is the entry
    `trusted_hops` from the right (`entries[len - hops]`). Picking a fixed depth
    from the right is what survives a multi-proxy chain: a single-proxy compose
    setup uses hops=1 (rightmost), while the ACA public-envoy→nginx→internal-
    envoy chain uses hops=3.

    If the chain is SHORTER than `trusted_hops`, the request did not traverse the
    expected proxy stack, so the whole XFF header is untrustworthy → fall back to
    the socket peer (never `entries[0]`, the most spoofable position). Whitespace
    is tolerated and an IPv4 `:port` suffix stripped; a garbage candidate or a
    missing peer falls back to `"unknown"`.
    """
    xff = request.headers.get("x-forwarded-for")
    if xff and trusted_hops >= 1:
        entries = [e.strip() for e in xff.split(",")]
        if len(entries) >= trusted_hops:
            candidate = _strip_ipv4_port(entries[len(entries) - trusted_hops])
            if candidate and _is_ip(candidate):
                return candidate
    client = request.client
    if client is not None and client.host:
        return client.host
    return "unknown"


def _resolve_policy(
    path: str, bearer: str | None, ip: str, settings: Settings
) -> tuple[str, int, str]:
    """Resolve (class, per-minute limit, bucket key) for a request.

    Order matters: the webhook (machine) path is keyed per-IP EVEN when a bearer
    is present, so an orchestrator's callbacks share one bucket regardless of any
    token they carry. A known provider segment (adf/airflow/dbt) is folded into
    the webhook key so each provider gets an independent per-IP bucket (#785).
    Otherwise a bearer buckets per sha256(token) and the unauthenticated path per
    client-IP. The raw token is never used as a key — only its hash — so it is
    never logged or stored.
    """
    if path.startswith(_WEBHOOK_PREFIX):
        provider = path[len(_WEBHOOK_PREFIX) :].split("/", 1)[0]
        prefix = f"{provider}:" if provider in _WEBHOOK_PROVIDERS else ""
        return "webhook", settings.rate_limit_webhook_per_minute, f"{prefix}ip:{ip}"
    if bearer is not None:
        digest = hashlib.sha256(bearer.encode()).hexdigest()[:32]
        return "default", settings.rate_limit_authenticated_per_minute, f"tok:{digest}"
    return "unauth", settings.rate_limit_unauthenticated_per_minute, f"ip:{ip}"


def _warn_store_unavailable_once(window: int, *, path: str, cls: str, key: str) -> None:
    global _store_unavailable_warned_window
    if _store_unavailable_warned_window == window:
        return

def _bucket_ip(ip: str, settings: Settings) -> str:
    """The per-IP bucket key component: `ip` folded onto its address prefix (#789).

    Keying per full /32 dilutes the cap against a rotating NAT/proxy pool — the
    pool spreads a burst across many sibling addresses so no single bucket ever
    fills (observed live: 200 requests over 11 distinct IPs in one /24, none near
    the cap). Folding onto a configurable prefix (IPv4 `rate_limit_ipv4_prefix`,
    default /24; IPv6 `rate_limit_ipv6_prefix`, default /64 — one subscriber's
    standard allocation) makes the whole pool share one bucket. The prefix length
    rides in the key, so retuning the mask starts fresh buckets rather than
    cross-counting. A non-address (`"unknown"`) passes through unchanged.
    """
    try:
        addr = ipaddress.ip_address(ip)
    except ValueError:
        return ip
    prefix = (
        settings.rate_limit_ipv4_prefix
        if isinstance(addr, ipaddress.IPv4Address)
        else settings.rate_limit_ipv6_prefix
    )
    network = ipaddress.ip_network((addr, prefix), strict=False)
    return f"{network.network_address}/{prefix}"


def _resolve_policy(
    path: str, bearer: str | None, ip: str, settings: Settings
) -> tuple[str, int, str]:

316	    now = int(_now())
317	    window = now // WINDOW_SECONDS
318	
319	    # The `default` (bearer) class gets a SECOND per-IP `ipall` bucket that counts
320	    # all bearer traffic from one IP, closing the rotated-token bypass (a fresh
321	    # random bearer mints a fresh tok bucket but shares the ipall ceiling). The
322	    # `webhook` class gets the same dual-key shape (#785): its primary bucket is
323	    # per provider + IP, so without an `ipall` ceiling one IP could spend
324	    # (providers + 1) x the webhook cap by rotating the segment. The unauth class
325	    # stays single-key — it is already per-IP on its one bucket.
326	    keys = [f"rl:{cls}:{key}:{window}"]
327	    if cls == "default":
328	        keys.append(f"rl:default:ipall:{ip}:{window}")
329	    elif cls == "webhook":
330	        keys.append(f"rl:webhook:ipall:{ip}:{window}")
331	    counts = await _active_store().incr_windows(keys)
332	
333	    if counts is None:
334	        # Fail-open: store unavailable → allow, warn once per window.
335	        _warn_store_unavailable_once(window, path=path, cls=cls, key=key)
336	        return await call_next(request)
337	
338	    # Primary bucket checked first so it wins the reported limit when both exceed.
339	    exceeded_limit: int | None = None
340	    if counts[0] > limit:
341	        exceeded_limit = limit
342	    elif cls == "default" and counts[1] > settings.rate_limit_ip_per_minute:
343	        exceeded_limit = settings.rate_limit_ip_per_minute
344	    elif cls == "webhook" and counts[1] > settings.rate_limit_webhook_ip_per_minute:
345	        exceeded_limit = settings.rate_limit_webhook_ip_per_minute

# ───────────────────────── policy resolution ─────────────────────────


def test_webhook_prefix_wins_even_with_bearer() -> None:
    s = _settings()
    cls, limit, key = _resolve_policy("/api/v1/orchestration/events/adf", "sometoken", "9.9.9.9", s)
    assert cls == "webhook"
    assert limit == s.rate_limit_webhook_per_minute
    assert key == "adf:ip:9.9.9.9"  # per-IP even though a bearer was present


@pytest.mark.parametrize("provider", sorted(rate_limit._WEBHOOK_PROVIDERS))
def test_webhook_key_folds_known_provider(provider: str) -> None:
    # Each provider gets its own per-IP bucket (#785), so a burst on one can't
    # crowd out another's callbacks from the same egress IP. Parametrized over
    # the production set so a newly added provider is exercised automatically.
    _, _, key = _resolve_policy(
        f"/api/v1/orchestration/events/{provider}", None, "9.9.9.9", _settings()
    )
    assert key == f"{provider}:ip:9.9.9.9"


def test_webhook_key_trailing_path_still_folds_provider() -> None:
    _, _, key = _resolve_policy(
        "/api/v1/orchestration/events/adf/extra", None, "9.9.9.9", _settings()
    )
    assert key == "adf:ip:9.9.9.9"


@pytest.mark.parametrize("segment", ["nonesuch", "", "adf\x00", "ADF"])
def test_webhook_unknown_segment_shares_bare_ip_bucket(segment: str) -> None:
    # NB: ASGI hands the middleware the percent-DECODED path, so an encoded
    # probe like `adf%00` arrives here as the raw "adf\x00" — test that layer.
    # Unknown segments must NOT mint fresh buckets (a scanner rotating the path
    # would never 429) — they all share the bare per-IP bucket.
    _, _, key = _resolve_policy(
        f"/api/v1/orchestration/events/{segment}", None, "9.9.9.9", _settings()
    )
    assert key == "ip:9.9.9.9"


def test_webhook_providers_match_orchestration_registry() -> None:
    # `_WEBHOOK_PROVIDERS` derives from the shared `db.models.ORCHESTRATION_PROVIDERS`
    # vocabulary; this pins that vocabulary to the orchestration registry so a
    # provider registered there can't silently land in the shared bare-IP bucket.
    from backend.app.orchestration.registry import _PROVIDERS

    assert rate_limit._WEBHOOK_PROVIDERS == frozenset(_PROVIDERS)


def test_bearer_keys_by_sha256_prefix() -> None:
    s = _settings()
    cls, limit, key = _resolve_policy("/api/v1/suites", "sometoken", "9.9.9.9", s)
    assert cls == "default"
    assert limit == s.rate_limit_authenticated_per_minute
    assert key.startswith("tok:")
    digest = key.removeprefix("tok:")
    assert len(digest) == 32
    assert all(c in "0123456789abcdef" for c in digest)
    assert digest == hashlib.sha256(b"sometoken").hexdigest()[:32]


def test_raw_token_never_in_key() -> None:
    _, _, key = _resolve_policy("/api/v1/suites", "super-secret-token", "9.9.9.9", _settings())
    assert "super-secret-token" not in key


def test_no_bearer_keys_by_ip() -> None:
    s = _settings()
    cls, limit, key = _resolve_policy("/api/v1/suites", None, "9.9.9.9", s)
    assert cls == "unauth"
    assert limit == s.rate_limit_unauthenticated_per_minute
    assert key == "ip:9.9.9.9"


# ───────────────────────── client-IP extraction ─────────────────────────


def test_client_ip_multi_hop_takes_last() -> None:
    # hops=1 (default) → the rightmost entry.

# ───────────────────────── prefix bucketing (#789) ─────────────────────────


def test_bucket_ip_ipv4_folds_to_slash24() -> None:
    # NAT/proxy-pool dilution: sibling /32s in one /24 must share a bucket.
    s = _settings()
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("2.57.171.13", s) == "2.57.171.0/24"
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("2.57.171.201", s) == "2.57.171.0/24"
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("2.57.172.13", s) == "2.57.172.0/24"  # next /24 is distinct


def test_bucket_ip_ipv6_folds_to_slash64() -> None:
    s = _settings()
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("2001:db8:1:2:aaaa::1", s) == "2001:db8:1:2::/64"
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("2001:db8:1:2:bbbb::2", s) == "2001:db8:1:2::/64"
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("2001:db8:1:3::1", s) == "2001:db8:1:3::/64"


def test_bucket_ip_full_mask_disables_grouping() -> None:
    s = Settings(_env_file=None, rate_limit_ipv4_prefix=32, rate_limit_ipv6_prefix=128)
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("9.9.9.9", s) == "9.9.9.9/32"
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("2001:db8::1", s) == "2001:db8::1/128"


def test_bucket_ip_non_address_passes_through() -> None:
    # `_client_ip` can yield "unknown" (no socket peer); it must key as-is, not raise.
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("unknown", _settings()) == "unknown"


def test_prefix_out_of_range_rejected_by_settings() -> None:
    with pytest.raises(Exception, match="rate_limit_ipv4_prefix"):
        Settings(_env_file=None, rate_limit_ipv4_prefix=0)

def test_sibling_ips_in_one_slash24_share_a_bucket(limiter: TestClient) -> None:
    # #789: a rotating NAT/proxy pool spreads a burst across sibling /32s in one
    # /24 — those must accumulate in ONE bucket and trip the cap together.
    for i in range(3):  # UNAUTH limit = 3
        h = {"X-Forwarded-For": f"9.9.9.{10 + i}"}  # three distinct /32s, one /24
        assert limiter.get(PROBE, headers=h).status_code != 429
    resp = limiter.get(PROBE, headers={"X-Forwarded-For": "9.9.9.99"})
    assert resp.status_code == 429
    # A different /24 is a fresh, independent bucket.
    assert limiter.get(PROBE, headers={"X-Forwarded-For": "9.9.8.1"}).status_code != 429

input.old_string: ### Threat model — the client-IP rule
input.new_string:
### Threat model — per-IP means per address prefix (#789)

Every per-IP bucket (unauth, webhook, and both `ipall` ceilings) keys on an
address **prefix** — IPv4 `/24`, IPv6 `/64` by default
(`RATE_LIMIT_IPV4_PREFIX` / `RATE_LIMIT_IPV6_PREFIX`) — not the full address.
Keying per `/32` dilutes the cap against a **rotating NAT/proxy pool**: the
post-#725 deploy verification observed a 200-request burst from one machine land
on 11 distinct source IPs inside a single `2.57.171.0/24` pool (13–26 requests
each, none near the 120 cap). The same mechanics serve a deliberately
distributed low-rate attacker. Folding onto the allocation prefix makes the pool
share one bucket; `/64` is the standard single-subscriber IPv6 allocation, where
per-address keying would be trivially dilutable (one host holds 2^64 addresses).
Trade-offs, accepted: a prefix that legitimately hosts many independent clients
(CGNAT, corporate egress) shares the cap — the masks are tunable per deployment,
and `/32` / `/128` disable grouping; benign CGNAT users are unaffected in the
default posture (they previously shared a single egress /32 anyway). The prefix
length rides in the key (`ip:2.57.171.0/24`), so retuning starts fresh buckets
instead of cross-counting, and the token-bucket class is IP-independent and
untouched.

### Threat model — the client-IP rule

Keying per `/32` dilutes the cap against a **rotating NAT/proxy pool**: the

Trade-offs, accepted: a prefix that legitimately hosts many independent clients
(CGNAT, corporate egress) shares the cap — the masks are tunable per deployment,
and `/32` / `/128` disable grouping; benign CGNAT users are unaffected in the

### Threat model — the client-IP rule
